kids.question: Where did Job live?
kids.option_a: The land of Uz
kids.option_b: The land of Canaan
kids.option_c: The land of Egypt
kids.option_d: The land of Nod
kids.explanation: The very first verse tells us: "There was a man in the land of Uz, whose name was Job" (Job 1:1).

kids.question: What did Job do every morning for his children?
kids.option_a: Gave them gifts
kids.option_b: Offered burnt offerings for them
kids.option_c: Taught them the law
kids.option_d: Prayed with them at the altar
kids.explanation: Job rose up early in the morning, and offered burnt offerings according to the number of his children (Job 1:5).

kids.question: What did Job say after he lost everything?
kids.option_a: The LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away
kids.option_b: I am ruined and destroyed
kids.option_c: Why hath God done this to me
kids.option_d: I will seek the LORD no more
kids.explanation: Job said, "The LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD" (Job 1:21).

teens.question: How is Job described at the very beginning of the book?
teens.option_a: Rich and powerful in Egypt
teens.option_b: Perfect and upright, one that feared God and eschewed evil
teens.option_c: A prophet and a priest in Israel
teens.option_d: A warrior and a king in Uz
teens.explanation: Job was "perfect and upright, and one that feared God, and eschewed evil" (Job 1:1).

teens.question: What did Satan claim was the reason Job feared God?
teens.option_a: Job was too ignorant to know better
teens.option_b: God had blessed and protected him with a hedge
teens.option_c: Job had made a covenant with God
teens.option_d: Job feared the punishment of death
teens.explanation: Satan said, "Hast not thou made an hedge about him...? but put forth thine hand now, and touch all that he hath" (Job 1:10-11).

teens.question: Which group of raiders stole Job's oxen and asses and killed his servants?
teens.option_a: The Philistines
teens.option_b: The Sabeans
teens.option_c: The Chaldeans
teens.option_d: The Amalekites
teens.explanation: A messenger told Job, "The Sabeans fell upon them, and took them away; yea, they have slain the servants with the edge of the sword" (Job 1:15).

teens.question: What happened to Job's sons and daughters?
teens.option_a: They fled to another land
teens.option_b: A great wind struck the house and they died
teens.option_c: They were taken captive by the Chaldeans
teens.option_d: They fell sick and died of a plague
teens.explanation: A great wind smote the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young men, and they are dead (Job 1:19).

teens.question: What did Job do immediately after hearing of all his losses?
teens.option_a: He sat in silence for seven days
teens.option_b: He rent his mantle, shaved his head, fell down, and worshipped
teens.option_c: He went to the temple to pray
teens.option_d: He cursed the day he was born
teens.explanation: Job rent his mantle, and shaved his head, and fell down upon the ground, and worshipped (Job 1:20).

adults.question: How many children, livestock categories, and servants made Job the greatest man in the East?
adults.option_a: Three children, four herds, five hundred servants
adults.option_b: Ten children; 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen, 500 she asses; a very great household
adults.option_c: Seven sons, five daughters, ten thousand cattle
adults.option_d: Twelve sons, great flocks, and a hundred servants
adults.explanation: Job had seven sons, three daughters, 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen, 500 she asses, and a very great household (Job 1:2-3).

adults.question: What was the occasion when Satan appeared before God in Job chapter 1?
adults.option_a: The Feast of Tabernacles
adults.option_b: When the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD
adults.option_c: The Day of Atonement
adults.option_d: When God summoned the angels to judgment
adults.explanation: There was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and Satan came also among them (Job 1:6).

adults.question: What specific question did the LORD ask Satan in Job 1:8?
adults.option_a: Why hast thou come before me?
adults.option_b: Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth?
adults.option_c: Where hast thou been that thou knowest my servant?
adults.option_d: How dost thou regard my servant Job?
adults.explanation: The LORD said unto Satan, "Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man?" (Job 1:8).

adults.question: What limitation did God place on Satan when He permitted him to test Job?
adults.option_a: Satan could not leave the land of Uz
adults.option_b: Satan could not touch any of Job's property
adults.option_c: Satan could only afflict Job's livestock
adults.option_d: Only upon himself put not forth thine hand — Job's person was protected
adults.explanation: The LORD said, "Behold, all that he hath is in thy power; only upon himself put not forth thine hand" (Job 1:12).

adults.question: How many separate messengers brought news of disaster to Job, and what made each message worse?
adults.option_a: Two, each worse than the last
adults.option_b: Four, each arriving before the previous had finished speaking
adults.option_c: Three, arriving on three consecutive days
adults.option_d: Five, one for each disaster
adults.explanation: Four messengers came, each saying "I only am escaped to tell thee," each arriving before the last finished speaking (Job 1:14-19).

adults.question: What disaster specifically destroyed Job's sheep and the servants tending them?
adults.option_a: A raid by the Sabeans
adults.option_b: A whirlwind from the wilderness
adults.option_c: The fire of God fell from heaven
adults.option_d: The Chaldeans took them in three bands
adults.explanation: "The fire of God is fallen from heaven, and hath burned up the sheep, and the servants, and consumed them" (Job 1:16).

adults.question: Who took Job's camels and slew his servants with the sword?
adults.option_a: The Sabeans
adults.option_b: A great whirlwind
adults.option_c: The Chaldeans in three bands
adults.option_d: Soldiers from the east
adults.explanation: "The Chaldeans made out three bands, and fell upon the camels, and have carried them away, yea, and slain the servants with the edge of the sword" (Job 1:17).

adults.question: What theological truth does the narrator affirm in Job 1:22 regarding Job's response to his suffering?
adults.option_a: Job was confused and did not know what to believe
adults.option_b: In all this Job sinned not, nor charged God foolishly
adults.option_c: Job's faith was shaken but he recovered
adults.option_d: Job immediately understood it was a test from God
adults.explanation: "In all this Job sinned not, nor charged God foolishly" (Job 1:22), affirming Job's integrity under extreme trial.

kids.question: What did Satan afflict Job with after the second meeting with God?
kids.option_a: Blindness
kids.option_b: Sore boils from head to foot
kids.option_c: A fever and great thirst
kids.option_d: Lameness in both legs
kids.explanation: Satan smote Job with sore boils from the sole of his foot unto his crown (Job 2:7).

kids.question: What did Job use to scrape his sores?
kids.option_a: A cloth
kids.option_b: A potsherd
kids.option_c: A stone
kids.option_d: A piece of wood
kids.explanation: Job took a potsherd to scrape himself; and he sat down among the ashes (Job 2:8).

kids.question: How many friends came to comfort Job?
kids.option_a: Two
kids.option_b: Three
kids.option_c: Four
kids.option_d: Five
kids.explanation: Three friends heard of all the evil that had come upon Job: Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar (Job 2:11).

teens.question: What did Job's wife tell him to do in his suffering?
teens.option_a: Pray without ceasing
teens.option_b: Curse God and die
teens.option_c: Flee to another land
teens.option_d: Offer a sacrifice
teens.explanation: Job's wife said unto him, "Dost thou still retain thine integrity? curse God, and die" (Job 2:9).

teens.question: How did Job rebuke his wife's advice?
teens.option_a: He told her to be silent and pray
teens.option_b: He called her one of the foolish women and said we must receive both good and evil from God
teens.option_c: He wept and refused to answer her
teens.option_d: He sent her away from his sight
teens.explanation: Job said, "Thou speakest as one of the foolish women speaketh...shall we not receive evil?" (Job 2:10).

teens.question: What are the names of Job's three friends who came to him?
teens.option_a: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
teens.option_b: Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar
teens.option_c: Nathan, Gad, and Samuel
teens.option_d: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego
teens.explanation: Now when Job's three friends heard...Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite (Job 2:11).

teens.question: What did Job's friends do when they saw him from afar?
teens.option_a: They ran to embrace him
teens.option_b: They lifted up their voice and wept, rent their mantles, and sprinkled dust on their heads
teens.option_c: They turned back in fear
teens.option_d: They brought him food and medicine
teens.explanation: They lifted up their voice, and wept; and they rent every one his mantle, and sprinkled dust upon their heads toward heaven (Job 2:12).

teens.question: How long did Job's friends sit with him in silence before anyone spoke?
teens.option_a: One day
teens.option_b: Three days and three nights
teens.option_c: Seven days and seven nights
teens.option_d: Forty days
teens.explanation: They sat down with him upon the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spake a word unto him (Job 2:13).

adults.question: What phrase did God use a second time to commend Job before Satan in chapter 2?
adults.option_a: He is a man after mine own heart
adults.option_b: Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth
adults.option_c: My servant Job hath done well in all things
adults.option_d: Job standeth perfect before me
adults.explanation: The LORD said unto Satan, "Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man?" (Job 2:3).

adults.question: What accusation did God level at Satan in Job 2:3 regarding Job's suffering in chapter 1?
adults.option_a: Satan had exceeded his authority
adults.option_b: Satan had moved God against Job without cause
adults.option_c: Satan had cheated in the wager
adults.option_d: Satan had lied about Job's faithfulness
adults.explanation: God said, "thou movedst me against him, to destroy him without cause" (Job 2:3).

adults.question: What is the meaning of Satan's reply "Skin for skin" in Job 2:4?
adults.option_a: Job would trade another's skin to save his own life
adults.option_b: Job would give up everything to preserve his own life
adults.option_c: Job's outer suffering had not yet touched his inner self
adults.option_d: Suffering increases in layers like skin upon skin
adults.explanation: "Skin for skin, yea, all that a man hath will he give for his life" (Job 2:4) — Satan argues Job will sacrifice anything to preserve himself.

adults.question: What new boundary did God set for Satan in Job 2:6?
adults.option_a: Satan could not touch his children
adults.option_b: Behold, he is in thine hand; but save his life
adults.option_c: Satan could only afflict him for thirty days
adults.option_d: Satan could not use disease
adults.explanation: The LORD said unto Satan, "Behold, he is in thine hand; but save his life" (Job 2:6).

adults.question: Where did Job sit after being afflicted with boils?
adults.option_a: In his house among his family
adults.option_b: Among the ashes
adults.option_c: In the city gate
adults.option_d: In the field outside the city
adults.explanation: Job went and sat down among the ashes (Job 2:8).

adults.question: What does Job's response to his wife in Job 2:10 reveal about his theology of suffering?
adults.option_a: He believed suffering was always a punishment
adults.option_b: He accepted that God sovereignly sends both good and evil
adults.option_c: He thought God was not in control
adults.option_d: He was angry but suppressed it
adults.explanation: Job said, "What? shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?" (Job 2:10).

adults.question: From where did each of Job's three friends come respectively?
adults.option_a: Jerusalem, Bethlehem, and Nazareth
adults.option_b: Teman, Shuah, and Naamah
adults.option_c: Egypt, Babylon, and Persia
adults.option_d: Uz, Buz, and Chesed
adults.explanation: Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite came to mourn with Job (Job 2:11).

adults.question: What does the narrator affirm about Job's speech after Satan's second attack?
adults.option_a: Job cried out in bitterness and questioned God
adults.option_b: In all this did not Job sin with his lips
adults.option_c: Job was silent for seven days without any word
adults.option_d: Job confessed secret sins he had committed
adults.explanation: "In all this did not Job sin with his lips" (Job 2:10) — Job's verbal integrity was maintained even in extreme physical suffering.

kids.question: After the seven days of silence, what did Job do?
kids.option_a: He thanked God for his trials
kids.option_b: He cursed the day he was born
kids.option_c: He asked his friends to pray for him
kids.option_d: He offered a sacrifice to God
kids.explanation: After this opened Job his mouth, and cursed his day (Job 3:1).

kids.question: What did Job wish had happened to the night he was born?
kids.option_a: That it had been a great feast
kids.option_b: That darkness and gloom had covered it
kids.option_c: That a star had shone brightly
kids.option_d: That it had been a holy day
kids.explanation: Job said, "let that night be solitary, let no joyful voice come therein" and wished darkness upon it (Job 3:7).

kids.question: What did Job long for in his great sadness?
kids.option_a: More friends to comfort him
kids.option_b: For death and rest in the grave
kids.option_c: For God to speak to him
kids.option_d: For his family to return
kids.explanation: Job asked, "Why died I not from the womb?" longing for rest like those who lie down in the grave (Job 3:11, 17).

teens.question: What is the main subject of Job's opening speech in chapter 3?
teens.option_a: A prayer asking God to restore his wealth
teens.option_b: A lament cursing the day of his birth and wishing he had never lived
teens.option_c: A confession of sin to his three friends
teens.option_d: A poem praising God in the midst of suffering
teens.explanation: Job opened his mouth and cursed his day, saying "Let the day perish wherein I was born" (Job 3:1-3).

teens.question: What does Job wish had happened at the moment of his birth?
teens.option_a: That an angel had announced it
teens.option_b: That the day had perished and never been seen
teens.option_c: That his father had rejoiced greatly
teens.option_d: That God had marked it as holy
teens.explanation: Job said, "Let the day perish wherein I was born, and the night in which it was said, There is a man child conceived" (Job 3:3).

teens.question: What state does Job describe for those who die at birth or in infancy?
teens.option_a: They are lost forever in darkness
teens.option_b: They rest in peace, untroubled like kings in their graves
teens.option_c: They wander without rest
teens.option_d: They wait for the resurrection
teens.explanation: Job says the stillborn and those who never saw light rest like kings and counsellors who built desolate places (Job 3:13-16).

teens.question: Who does Job say finds light grievous and longs for death that does not come?
teens.option_a: Those who are in poverty
teens.option_b: Those whose way is hid, and whom God hath hedged in
teens.option_c: Those who have lost their children
teens.option_d: Those who have sinned greatly
teens.explanation: Job says those whose way is hid, and whom God hath hedged in, long for death but it cometh not (Job 3:23).

teens.question: What poetic image does Job use to describe the relentlessness of his sighing?
teens.option_a: My sighing is like the waves of the sea
teens.option_b: My sighing cometh before I eat, and my roarings are poured out like the waters
teens.option_c: My sighing fills the heavens above me
teens.option_d: My sighing is a burden too great to bear
teens.explanation: "For my sighing cometh before I eat, and my roarings are poured out like the waters" (Job 3:24).

adults.question: What is the literary form of Job's curse in chapter 3, and how does it mirror the creation account?
adults.option_a: It is a hymn that praises God's creation in reverse
adults.option_b: It is a reverse-creation lament calling for darkness and chaos to unmake the day of his birth
adults.option_c: It is a legal complaint structured as a court petition
adults.option_d: It is a wisdom poem comparing life to a shadow
adults.explanation: Job calls for darkness (3:4), cursing the light — an inversion of Genesis 1:3 — wishing uncreation on the day of his birth.

adults.question: What mythological figure is referenced in Job 3:8 as one who could curse the day?
adults.option_a: Leviathan
adults.option_b: Those who curse the day, who are ready to rouse up Leviathan
adults.option_c: The serpent of chaos
adults.option_d: The ancient dragon
adults.explanation: "Let them curse it that curse the day, who are ready to raise up their mourning" — KJV margin reads those ready to raise up Leviathan (Job 3:8).

adults.question: What does Job mean when he says in Job 3:23 that his way "is hid" and God "hath hedged him in"?
adults.option_a: Job could not see the road in front of him
adults.option_b: Job felt trapped by God's providence with no escape and no understanding of his situation
adults.option_c: Job was lost in the wilderness
adults.option_d: Job was confined to his house by his illness
adults.explanation: "Why is light given to a man whose way is hid, and whom God hath hedged in?" (Job 3:23) — Job feels providentially trapped with no way forward.

adults.question: What contrast does Job draw in Job 3:17-19 about the realm of the dead?
adults.option_a: The dead suffer more than the living
adults.option_b: In the grave the wicked cease troubling and the weary are at rest — all are equal regardless of status
adults.option_c: The righteous rest but the wicked are tormented
adults.option_d: Only kings and nobles find rest in death
adults.explanation: "There the wicked cease from troubling; and there the weary be at rest...the small and great are there" (Job 3:17-19).

adults.question: What does Job say in Job 3:25 about his fears?
adults.option_a: He feared God's wrath from his youth
adults.option_b: The thing which he greatly feared had come upon him
adults.option_c: He feared that his friends would abandon him
adults.option_d: He feared that he would lose his faith
adults.explanation: "For the thing which I greatly feared is come upon me, and that which I was afraid of is come unto me" (Job 3:25).

adults.question: How does Job describe the state of those who dig for death as for hidden treasure in Job 3:21?
adults.option_a: They die quickly and find rest
adults.option_b: They rejoice exceedingly and are glad when they find the grave
adults.option_c: They search in vain and are forgotten
adults.option_d: They are punished for seeking death
adults.explanation: "Which long for death, but it cometh not; and dig for it more than for hid treasures; Which rejoice exceedingly, and are glad, when they can find the grave?" (Job 3:21-22).

adults.question: What is the significance of Job's lament in chapter 3 in the overall structure of the book?
adults.option_a: It proves Job was secretly sinful
adults.option_b: It marks the transition from narrative prologue to the poetic dialogues and shows even the righteous may cry out in pain
adults.option_c: It is the climax of the book resolved by the friends' comfort
adults.option_d: It establishes Job as an unbeliever
adults.explanation: Chapter 3's lament opens the poetic dialogues and shows that authentic faith does not preclude anguished lamentation, setting the stage for the debates.

adults.question: What does the phrase "I was not in safety, neither had I rest, neither was I quiet; yet trouble came" (Job 3:26) communicate?
adults.option_a: Job had been careless before his sufferings
adults.option_b: Even in previous peace Job had no true security, and now suffering has overwhelmed all rest
adults.option_c: Job confesses he had sinned in prosperity
adults.option_d: Job admits he deserved the trouble that came
adults.explanation: "I was not in safety, neither had I rest, neither was I quiet; yet trouble came" (Job 3:26) — Job's peace was already fragile, making the sudden onslaught of suffering total.

kids.question: Who speaks first among Job's three friends?
kids.option_a: Bildad
kids.option_b: Zophar
kids.option_c: Eliphaz
kids.option_d: Elihu
kids.explanation: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said... (Job 4:1).

kids.question: What did Eliphaz say Job had done for others in the past?
kids.option_a: Taught them the law of Moses
kids.option_b: Instructed many and strengthened weak hands
kids.option_c: Built them houses and fed their families
kids.option_d: Led them in prayer every morning
kids.explanation: Eliphaz said, "Behold, thou hast instructed many, and thou hast strengthened the weak hands" (Job 4:3).

kids.question: According to Eliphaz, can a truly good person be destroyed?
kids.option_a: Yes, anyone can be destroyed
kids.option_b: No, the innocent are never destroyed
kids.option_c: Only if they stop praying
kids.option_d: Only if God wills it for a reason
kids.explanation: Eliphaz asks, "who ever perished, being innocent? or where were the righteous cut off?" (Job 4:7).

teens.question: What is the main argument Eliphaz makes in his first speech?
teens.option_a: Job is being tested by God and will be restored
teens.option_b: The innocent do not perish — suffering proves Job has sinned
teens.option_c: God is distant and does not concern himself with men
teens.option_d: Job should accept his suffering and make no complaint
teens.explanation: Eliphaz argues that "they that plow iniquity...reap the same" (Job 4:8), implying Job's suffering reveals hidden sin.

teens.question: What natural illustration does Eliphaz use to describe how the wicked perish?
teens.option_a: A tree that is cut down at the root
teens.option_b: The lion — its roaring, teeth, and whelps all perish
teens.option_c: A river that runs dry in summer
teens.option_d: A storm that passes over and is gone
teens.explanation: Eliphaz says the roaring of the lion and the teeth of the young lions are broken — a picture of the wicked's destruction (Job 4:10-11).

teens.question: How did Eliphaz receive the revelation he shares with Job?
teens.option_a: Through reading the scriptures
teens.option_b: A thing was secretly brought to him in a vision of the night
teens.option_c: Through the counsel of wise men
teens.option_d: An angel appeared to him in broad daylight
teens.explanation: "Now a thing was secretly brought to me, and mine ear received a little thereof. In thoughts from the visions of the night" (Job 4:12-13).

teens.question: What question does the spirit in Eliphaz's vision ask?
teens.option_a: Why dost thou complain against the Lord?
teens.option_b: Shall mortal man be more just than God? shall a man be more pure than his maker?
teens.option_c: Hast thou considered thy ways before the LORD?
teens.option_d: Who art thou that rebukes the Almighty?
teens.explanation: "Shall mortal man be more just than God? shall a man be more pure than his maker?" (Job 4:17).

teens.question: What does Eliphaz say about the angels in Job 4:18?
teens.option_a: The angels worship God perfectly without error
teens.option_b: God charged his angels with folly
teens.option_c: The angels are more righteous than man
teens.option_d: The angels cannot see the ways of men
teens.explanation: "Behold, he put no trust in his servants; and his angels he charged with folly" (Job 4:18).

adults.question: What does the phrase "thy fear" and "thy confidence" in Job 4:6 refer to in Eliphaz's opening appeal?
adults.option_a: Job's fear of death and confidence in his wealth
adults.option_b: Job's piety (fear of God) and his hope as an upright man
adults.option_c: Job's fear of his enemies and his self-confidence
adults.option_d: Job's trembling and his boasting
adults.explanation: Eliphaz says, "Is not this thy fear, thy confidence, thy hope, and the uprightness of thy ways?" (Job 4:6) — appealing to Job's own professed piety.

adults.question: What agricultural metaphor does Eliphaz use in Job 4:8 to describe the fate of the wicked?
adults.option_a: Those who plant thorns will harvest them
adults.option_b: They that plow iniquity, and sow wickedness, reap the same
adults.option_c: The field of the wicked is never watered
adults.option_d: The harvest of evil men is left to rot
adults.explanation: "Even as I have seen, they that plow iniquity, and sow wickedness, reap the same" (Job 4:8).

adults.question: What physical effect did the vision have on Eliphaz before the spirit spoke?
adults.option_a: He was filled with joy and peace
adults.option_b: Fear came upon him, trembling, which made all his bones shake
adults.option_c: He fell into a deep sleep and could not move
adults.option_d: He wept and tore his clothing
adults.explanation: "Fear came upon me, and trembling, which made all my bones to shake" (Job 4:14).

adults.question: How does Eliphaz describe the appearance of the spirit in his vision?
adults.option_a: A bright angel in white robes
adults.option_b: An image was before his eyes with still silence and a voice
adults.option_c: A pillar of fire and cloud
adults.option_d: A whirlwind that spoke with thunder
adults.explanation: "Then a spirit passed before my face; the hair of my flesh stood up...there was silence, and I heard a voice" (Job 4:15-16).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz say about humans who dwell in houses of clay in Job 4:19-21?
adults.option_a: They are blessed by God because of their frailty
adults.option_b: Their foundation is in the dust; they are crushed before the moth and perish without wisdom
adults.option_c: They shall be raised up by God in the last day
adults.option_d: Their weakness makes them innocent before God
adults.explanation: "How much less in them that dwell in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, which are crushed before the moth...They are destroyed from morning to evening" (Job 4:19-20).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz mean by asking if Job's "hope" and "confidence" should be his source of strength (Job 4:6)?
adults.option_a: He is encouraging Job to rely on optimism
adults.option_b: He uses Job's own stated piety as evidence that Job should be able to bear this, implying Job's complaint undermines his claim of righteousness
adults.option_c: He is complimenting Job on his great faith
adults.option_d: He reminds Job that wealth will return
adults.explanation: Eliphaz subtly argues that if Job were truly righteous (as he claims), his piety itself should sustain him — making Job's lament look hypocritical (Job 4:6).

adults.question: In the context of the lion imagery in Job 4:10-11, what theological point is Eliphaz making?
adults.option_a: Wild animals are more dangerous than humans
adults.option_b: Even the fiercest and most powerful wicked man is ultimately destroyed
adults.option_c: God protects the righteous from lions
adults.option_d: Lions are a symbol of God's wrath against nations
adults.explanation: The roaring lion, old lion, stout lion cubs, and lion's whelps all perish — a graduated image of how completely the wicked are wiped out (Job 4:10-11).

adults.question: What is the theological problem with Eliphaz's vision-based argument that God charges even angels with folly (Job 4:18)?
adults.option_a: Angels do not exist in Eliphaz's worldview
adults.option_b: He uses it to argue that Job, being merely human, must certainly be impure — but this denies Job's God-attested integrity
adults.option_c: The vision was from a demonic source
adults.option_d: Eliphaz misquotes the scripture he received
adults.explanation: Eliphaz argues from the angels' impurity downward to human impurity (Job 4:17-19), but this ignores the fact that God Himself twice declared Job blameless (Job 1:8; 2:3).

kids.question: What does Eliphaz say God does for the poor?
kids.option_a: God ignores the poor until they repent
kids.option_b: God saveth the poor from the sword and from their mouth
kids.option_c: God gives the poor riches if they are faithful
kids.option_d: God sends angels to protect the poor
kids.explanation: Eliphaz says God "saveth the poor from the sword, from their mouth, and from the hand of the mighty" (Job 5:15).

kids.question: According to Eliphaz, what does God do to the humble?
kids.option_a: He leaves them in their trouble
kids.option_b: He sets on high those who mourn
kids.option_c: He rewards them with children
kids.option_d: He speaks to them in dreams
kids.explanation: Eliphaz says God "setteth up on high those that be low; that those which mourn may be exalted to safety" (Job 5:11).

kids.question: What does Eliphaz say is true of the man whom God corrects?
kids.option_a: He will suffer forever
kids.option_b: He is a happy man
kids.option_c: He is forgotten by God
kids.option_d: He must offer more sacrifices
kids.explanation: Eliphaz says "Behold, happy is the man whom God correcteth: therefore despise not thou the chastening of the Almighty" (Job 5:17).

teens.question: What does Eliphaz say about calling to the holy ones (saints) for help?
teens.option_a: They will immediately answer the righteous
teens.option_b: There is no one among the holy ones to answer Job — he should turn to God
teens.option_c: The holy ones intercede before God for the afflicted
teens.option_d: They are more powerful than men but less than God
teens.explanation: Eliphaz asks, "Call now, if there be any that will answer thee; and to which of the saints wilt thou turn?" (Job 5:1).

teens.question: What does Eliphaz say happens to the foolish man who takes root?
teens.option_a: His house is blessed with peace
teens.option_b: He is suddenly cursed and his children are far from safety
teens.option_c: He grows wealthy for a season before he is cut off
teens.option_d: He repents and is restored
teens.explanation: Eliphaz says, "Wrath killeth the foolish man, and envy slayeth the silly one...his children are far from safety" (Job 5:2-4).

teens.question: What does Eliphaz claim about the origin of trouble and affliction?
teens.option_a: It comes from Satan alone
teens.option_b: Affliction springeth not out of the dust, neither doth trouble spring out of the ground — man brings it on himself
teens.option_c: It comes randomly with no cause
teens.option_d: God sends it only as punishment for great sins
teens.explanation: "Yet man is born unto trouble, as the sparks fly upward" and "affliction springeth not out of the dust" (Job 5:6-7) — Eliphaz says man himself is the cause.

teens.question: What does Eliphaz promise Job if he seeks God?
teens.option_a: That God will immediately give him wealth
teens.option_b: That God will do great and marvellous things, giving rain and setting the humble on high
teens.option_c: That his friends will remain with him
teens.option_d: That his children will be restored in one day
teens.explanation: Eliphaz urges Job to seek God "which doeth great things and unsearchable; marvellous things without number" including sending rain and lifting the low (Job 5:8-11).

teens.question: What comprehensive blessing does Eliphaz promise will come to Job if he returns to God?
teens.option_a: Wealth, fame, and long life among kings
teens.option_b: Protection at home and in the field, peace with beasts and stones, many children, and a full life
teens.option_c: Healing of his boils and restoration of his cattle
teens.option_d: A vision of God and understanding of all mysteries
teens.explanation: Eliphaz promises safety at home, peace with wild animals, fruitfulness of fields, many descendants, and coming to the grave in full age like a shock of corn (Job 5:23-26).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz mean in Job 5:2 when he says "wrath killeth the foolish man, and envy slayeth the silly one"?
adults.option_a: Anger and jealousy are physical diseases
adults.option_b: The passionate impatience of the fool — resentment and envy toward God's dealings — destroys him
adults.option_c: Foolish men are killed in wars caused by anger
adults.option_d: Silly people envy the wise and suffer for it
adults.explanation: Eliphaz implies that Job's angry lament (ch. 3) is itself the mark of a fool whose passionate resentment will destroy him (Job 5:2).

adults.question: What does the harvest imagery in Job 5:5 communicate about the fate of the foolish man's family?
adults.option_a: Their crops are plentiful but stolen
adults.option_b: Even their harvest is eaten by strangers and the hungry, and their substance is taken
adults.option_c: Their children will inherit great wealth
adults.option_d: Their land becomes permanently barren
adults.explanation: "Whose harvest the hungry eateth up, and taketh it even out of the thorns, and the robber swalloweth up their substance" (Job 5:5).

adults.question: How does Eliphaz describe God's frustration of the devices of the crafty in Job 5:12-13?
adults.option_a: God ignores their plans and leaves them alone
adults.option_b: He disappointeth the devices of the crafty so their hands cannot perform enterprise, and He taketh the wise in their own craftiness
adults.option_c: He punishes them with sickness
adults.option_d: He sends angels to prevent their plans
adults.explanation: "He disappointeth the devices of the crafty...He taketh the wise in their own craftiness" (Job 5:12-13).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz say about God's relationship with the poor and needy in Job 5:15-16?
adults.option_a: God tests the poor before helping them
adults.option_b: God saveth the poor from the sword and from the hand of the mighty, and the poor hath hope
adults.option_c: God rewards the poor only after they repent
adults.option_d: The poor must call on the saints for intercession first
adults.explanation: "He saveth the poor from the sword, from their mouth, and from the hand of the mighty. So the poor hath hope, and iniquity stoppeth her mouth" (Job 5:15-16).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz promise in Job 5:18 about God's discipline — that He wounds and also does what?
adults.option_a: He forgets and lets the wound fester
adults.option_b: He bindeth up, and his hands make whole
adults.option_c: He replaces what was lost
adults.option_d: He removes the memory of pain
adults.explanation: "For he maketh sore, and bindeth up: he woundeth, and his hands make whole" (Job 5:18).

adults.question: How many calamities does Eliphaz say God will deliver Job from in Job 5:19?
adults.option_a: Three
adults.option_b: Five
adults.option_c: Six, and in seven no evil shall touch him
adults.option_d: Ten
adults.explanation: "He shall deliver thee in six troubles: yea, in seven there shall no evil touch thee" (Job 5:19) — a numerical saying emphasizing complete protection.

adults.question: What covenant does Eliphaz promise Job will make in Job 5:23 if he is restored?
adults.option_a: A covenant with the sons of Abraham
adults.option_b: A covenant with the stones of the field and with the beasts of the field
adults.option_c: A covenant of peace with his enemies
adults.option_d: A covenant with God at the altar
adults.explanation: "For thou shalt be in league with the stones of the field: and the beasts of the field shall be at peace with thee" (Job 5:23).

adults.question: How does Eliphaz conclude his speech in Job 5:27, and what does this claim reveal about his method?
adults.option_a: He quotes from the law of Moses as his authority
adults.option_b: He appeals to collective wisdom: "we have searched it, so it is; hear it, and know thou it for thy good"
adults.option_c: He invites Job to verify his words with a priest
adults.option_d: He confesses that he may be wrong and asks Job to correct him
adults.explanation: "Lo this, we have searched it, so it is; hear it, and know thou it for thy good" (Job 5:27) — Eliphaz relies on the authority of traditional wisdom rather than divine revelation.

kids.question: What does Job say is heavier than the sand of the sea?
kids.option_a: His illness
kids.option_b: His grief and calamity
kids.option_c: His loss of wealth
kids.option_d: His longing for death
kids.explanation: Job says "my grief were throughly weighed...it would be heavier than the sand of the sea" (Job 6:2-3).

kids.question: What does Job compare his friends to in this chapter?
kids.option_a: Strong lions that protect the weak
kids.option_b: A deceitful brook that dries up
kids.option_c: A fruitful garden that feeds the hungry
kids.option_d: A bright light in the darkness
kids.explanation: Job says his brethren are like a brook, like streams of brooks that pass away (Job 6:15).

kids.question: What does Job ask his friends to do instead of just criticising him?
kids.option_a: To pray to God on his behalf
kids.option_b: To teach him and show him where he has gone wrong
kids.option_c: To leave him alone in peace
kids.option_d: To bring him food and medicine
kids.explanation: Job says "Teach me, and I will hold my tongue: and cause me to understand wherein I have erred" (Job 6:24).

teens.question: What does Job say the arrows of the Almighty have done to him?
teens.option_a: They have strengthened him for battle
teens.option_b: Their poison drinketh up his spirit and the terrors of God are set against him
teens.option_c: They have passed over him without harm
teens.option_d: They have sharpened his understanding
teens.explanation: "For the arrows of the Almighty are within me, the poison whereof drinketh up my spirit: the terrors of God do set themselves in array against me" (Job 6:4).

teens.question: What does Job use in Job 6:6 to illustrate his point that his complaint is not without cause?
teens.option_a: A lamp that burns with no oil
teens.option_b: Unsavoury food — can that which is unsavoury be eaten without salt?
teens.option_c: A broken vessel that holds no water
teens.option_d: An ox that refuses to plough
teens.explanation: "Can that which is unsavoury be eaten without salt? or is there any taste in the white of an egg?" (Job 6:6) — Job argues that his lament is a natural response to his situation.

teens.question: What one thing does Job ask God to grant him?
teens.option_a: That God would restore his children
teens.option_b: That it would please God to destroy him and let him die
teens.option_c: That God would reveal the reason for his suffering
teens.option_d: That God would send a comforter
teens.explanation: "Even that it would please God to destroy me; that he would let loose his hand, and cut me off" (Job 6:9).

teens.question: What does Job say his friends have shown him, like a deceitful brook in summer?
teens.option_a: False loyalty and vain hope
teens.option_b: True wisdom but poor timing
teens.option_c: Great love but little understanding
teens.option_d: Correct doctrine but cold hearts
teens.explanation: Job says his brethren have dealt deceitfully as a brook: "My brethren have dealt deceitfully as a brook...ye see my casting down, and are afraid" (Job 6:15, 21).

teens.question: What challenge does Job make to his friends regarding his words in Job 6:25-26?
teens.option_a: He asks them to praise his patience
teens.option_b: He asks them to show him where he has erred, since honest words do not rebuke, but they only reprove his speeches as wind
teens.option_c: He dares them to prove their own righteousness
teens.option_d: He asks them to speak louder so others can hear
teens.explanation: "How forcible are right words! but what doth your arguing reprove? Do ye imagine to reprove words, and the speeches of one that is desperate, which are as wind?" (Job 6:25-26).

adults.question: What does Job mean in Job 6:10 when he says his consolation would be that "I have not concealed the words of the Holy One"?
adults.option_a: He hopes to receive a special revelation
adults.option_b: Even in mortal pain he takes comfort that he has remained faithful in speech and has not denied God's word
adults.option_c: He believes God will speak to him soon
adults.option_d: He is claiming perfect sinlessness
adults.explanation: "Then should I yet have comfort; yea, I would harden myself in sorrow: let him not spare; for I have not concealed the words of the Holy One" (Job 6:10).

adults.question: What does Job's reference to the strength of stones and brass in Job 6:12 communicate?
adults.option_a: He admires the permanence of creation
adults.option_b: He protests that he has no superhuman endurance — he is flesh, not stone or brass
adults.option_c: He praises God for making durable things
adults.option_d: He asks for strength like stone to endure longer
adults.explanation: "Is my strength the strength of stones? or is my flesh of brass?" (Job 6:12) — Job rejects Eliphaz's implied demand that he endure silently as if he had unlimited strength.

adults.question: What detailed geography is used for the deceitful brook in Job 6:17-20?
adults.option_a: It flows from Lebanon to the Dead Sea
adults.option_b: It is black with ice and snow in winter but vanishes in heat, leaving caravans of Tema and Sheba ashamed
adults.option_c: It rises in the spring but fails before the harvest
adults.option_d: It flows underground and cannot be found
adults.explanation: The brook is blackish with ice, snow melts into it, but in heat it vanishes, leaving the troops of Tema and Sheba confounded (Job 6:16-20).

adults.question: What specific accusation does Job level against his friends in Job 6:21 and 6:27?
adults.option_a: They are too young to understand suffering
adults.option_b: They have become nothing, afraid of his condition, and would even cast lots over the fatherless and dig a pit for a friend
adults.option_c: They have given him bad theological advice
adults.option_d: They have not prayed enough for his recovery
adults.explanation: "For now ye are nothing; ye see my casting down, and are afraid...Yea, ye overwhelm the fatherless, and ye dig a pit for your friend" (Job 6:21, 27).

adults.question: What commercial or ethical standard does Job invoke in Job 6:28-29 to demand fair treatment from his friends?
adults.option_a: The law of hospitality to guests
adults.option_b: He asks them to look at him directly, return again and his righteousness is at stake — it is worth returning
adults.option_c: The covenant of friendship sworn before God
adults.option_d: The tradition of the elders
adults.explanation: "Now therefore be content, look upon me; for it is evident unto you if I lie. Return, I pray you, let it not be iniquity; yea, return again, my righteousness is in it" (Job 6:28-29).

adults.question: What does Job say in Job 6:14 about the treatment he deserves from his friend?
adults.option_a: A friend should rebuke openly
adults.option_b: To him that is afflicted pity should be shewed from his friend, but he hath forsaken the fear of the Almighty
adults.option_c: A friend should offer sacrifices on his behalf
adults.option_d: A friend should speak truth even when it hurts
adults.explanation: "To him that is afflicted pity should be shewed from his friend; but he forsaketh the fear of the Almighty" (Job 6:14).

adults.question: What is the rhetorical force of Job's question "Did I say, Bring unto me? or, Give a reward for me?" in Job 6:22?
adults.option_a: Job is asking his friends to pay for his care
adults.option_b: Job argues he asked nothing of them — making their cold treatment even more inexcusable
adults.option_c: Job is complaining that no one helped him financially
adults.option_d: Job demands they testify on his behalf
adults.explanation: "Did I say, Bring unto me? or, Give a reward for me of your substance?" (Job 6:22) — Job shows he asked for nothing material, heightening the failure of mere compassion.

adults.question: What does Job's closing question in Job 6:30 assert about his moral integrity?
adults.option_a: He confesses he may have sinned in his speech
adults.option_b: He asks whether there is iniquity in his tongue and whether his taste cannot discern perverse things — asserting he can tell right from wrong
adults.option_c: He admits his lament was excessive
adults.option_d: He asks his friends to judge him fairly
adults.explanation: "Is there iniquity in my tongue? cannot my taste discern perverse things?" (Job 6:30) — Job closes by reasserting his moral discernment and the legitimacy of his complaint.

kids.question: What does Job compare his life to in chapter 7?
kids.option_a: A tall mountain that stands forever
kids.option_b: A wind that passes away
kids.option_c: A river that never runs dry
kids.option_d: A strong tree in the forest
kids.explanation: Job says "my life is wind" and "mine eye shall no more see good" (Job 7:7).

kids.question: What does Job say happens to a cloud?
kids.option_a: It brings rain and refreshes the earth
kids.option_b: It consumeth and vanisheth away and goes down to the grave no more
kids.option_c: It stays in the sky forever
kids.option_d: It becomes the dew of the morning
kids.explanation: Job says "As the cloud is consumed and vanisheth away: so he that goeth down to the grave shall come up no more" (Job 7:9).

kids.question: Why does Job say God watches him so closely?
kids.option_a: He does not know and asks why God sets him as a target
kids.option_b: Because Job is a great sinner who must be corrected
kids.option_c: Because God loves Job more than anyone else
kids.option_d: Because Job asked God to watch over him
kids.explanation: Job asks "Am I a sea, or a whale, that thou settest a watch over me?" (Job 7:12) — he does not understand why God targets him.

teens.question: What illustration does Job use at the start of chapter 7 to describe the misery of human life?
teens.option_a: A sparrow fallen to the ground
teens.option_b: An appointed time of hard service — like a hireling who longs for his wages
teens.option_c: A prisoner who waits for pardon
teens.option_d: A lamp that is almost burnt out
teens.explanation: "Is there not an appointed time to man upon earth? are not his days also like the days of an hireling?" (Job 7:1).

teens.question: How does Job describe his nights in Job 7:3-4?
teens.option_a: Long months of vanity and wearisome nights; lying down wishing for morning till the dawning
teens.option_b: Peaceful sleep with vivid dreams
teens.option_c: Short nights that pass too quickly
teens.option_d: Nights of prayer and meditation
teens.explanation: "So am I made to possess months of vanity, and wearisome nights are appointed to me...and the night be gone? and I am full of tossings to and fro unto the dawning of the day" (Job 7:3-4).

teens.question: What physical symptoms does Job describe in Job 7:5?
teens.option_a: Blindness and deafness from his grief
teens.option_b: His flesh clothed with worms and clods of dust, his skin broken and become loathsome
teens.option_c: Fever and chills that will not cease
teens.option_d: Weakness in his limbs so he cannot rise
teens.explanation: "My flesh is clothed with worms and clods of dust; my skin is broken, and become loathsome" (Job 7:5).

teens.question: What famous question does Job ask God in Job 7:17 that echoes Psalm 8?
teens.option_a: Why hast thou forsaken me?
teens.option_b: What is man, that thou shouldest magnify him? and that thou shouldest set thine heart upon him?
teens.option_c: How long wilt thou afflict me?
teens.option_d: Shall the dust praise thee?
teens.explanation: "What is man, that thou shouldest magnify him? and that thou shouldest set thine heart upon him?" (Job 7:17) — echoing Psalm 8:4 but in a very different tone.

teens.question: What does Job ask God to do in Job 7:19 regarding his suffering?
teens.option_a: Never leave his side
teens.option_b: Turn away from him and let him alone till he swallow down his spittle
teens.option_c: Reveal the reason for his pain
teens.option_d: Send his children back to him
teens.explanation: "How long wilt thou not depart from me, nor let me alone till I swallow down my spittle?" (Job 7:19).

adults.question: What double image of a slave and a hireling in Job 7:1-2 does Job use to describe the burden of human existence?
adults.option_a: Both describe total freedom and joy
adults.option_b: A slave desires the shadow — relief from labour; a hireling earnestly looks for his wages — both long for release from their toil
adults.option_c: A slave represents the wicked and a hireling the righteous
adults.option_d: Both images describe the reward of patience
adults.explanation: "As a servant earnestly desireth the shadow, and as an hireling looketh for the reward of his work" (Job 7:2) — both yearn for release from grinding toil.

adults.question: What does the imagery of the weaver's shuttle in Job 7:6 convey about Job's perception of his life?
adults.option_a: Life is intricately woven and beautiful
adults.option_b: His days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle, speeding to an end without hope
adults.option_c: Life requires patient craftsmanship
adults.option_d: The pattern of his life is determined by God
adults.explanation: "My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle, and are spent without hope" (Job 7:6).

adults.question: What does Job mean in Job 7:8-10 when he says the eye that sees him now will see him no more and he shall return no more to his house?
adults.option_a: He is planning to move to another city
adults.option_b: He anticipates the finality of death — the dead do not return, their place knows them no more
adults.option_c: He believes God will hide his face from him
adults.option_d: He expects to be sent into exile
adults.explanation: "The eye of him that hath seen me shall see me no more...He shall return no more to his house, neither shall his place know him any more" (Job 7:8-10) — a meditation on the irreversibility of death.

adults.question: In Job 7:12 Job asks "Am I a sea, or a whale, that thou settest a watch over me?" What is the theological irony of this question?
adults.option_a: Job is praising God for protecting creation from chaos
adults.option_b: Job compares himself to the primordial chaos monsters (yam/tannin) that God must restrain — ironic since Job is a righteous man, not a chaos force
adults.option_c: Job is asking to be given the power of the sea
adults.option_d: Job believes God has confused him with a wicked nation
adults.explanation: "Am I a sea, or a whale, that thou settest a watch over me?" (Job 7:12) — Job uses the ancient image of God containing chaotic sea monsters to question why he is being treated like a cosmic enemy.

adults.question: What does Job say about dreams and visions in Job 7:13-15?
adults.option_a: His dreams bring him comfort and rest
adults.option_b: When his bed should comfort him, God scares him with dreams and visions so that his soul chooseth strangling and death rather than his life
adults.option_c: He no longer dreams because God has taken that gift away
adults.option_d: His visions show him the path to restoration
adults.explanation: "When I say, My bed shall comfort me, my couch shall ease my complaint; Then thou scarest me with dreams, and terrifiest me through visions" (Job 7:13-14).

adults.question: What does Job mean in Job 7:20 by calling himself a burden to God?
adults.option_a: He believes he is too heavy for God to carry
adults.option_b: He sarcastically asks why, if he has sinned, he should be a burden to God — who misses nothing — questioning the proportionality of the response
adults.option_c: He confesses all his sins before God
adults.option_d: He asks God to lighten his punishment
adults.explanation: "I have sinned; what shall I do unto thee, O thou preserver of men? why hast thou set me as a mark against thee, so that I am a burden to myself?" (Job 7:20).

adults.question: How does Job's use of Psalm 8 language in Job 7:17-18 differ from the psalm's original intent?
adults.option_a: Both passages celebrate God's care for mankind equally
adults.option_b: Psalm 8 marvels with gratitude that God cares for small man; Job uses the same words to protest that God's attention is overwhelming and relentless rather than gracious
adults.option_c: Job quotes Psalm 8 to praise God for watching over him
adults.option_d: Job is unaware of Psalm 8 and the parallel is coincidental
adults.explanation: Psalm 8:4 celebrates divine attention as grace; Job 7:17-18 turns the same question into a protest: "what is man that thou...shouldest visit him every morning?"

adults.question: What urgent eschatological tone does Job strike in Job 7:21 by asking why God does not pardon his transgression?
adults.option_a: Job expects a long future with many chances for restoration
adults.option_b: Job says God must act soon because he will soon lie down in the dust and God will seek him diligently but he shall not be found — urging divine urgency before death ends the opportunity
adults.option_c: Job believes God will forgive him in the next life
adults.option_d: Job is threatening to stop worshipping if God does not act
adults.explanation: "Why dost thou not pardon my transgression...for now shall I sleep in the dust; and thou shalt seek me in the morning, but I shall not be" (Job 7:21).

kids.question: Which of Job's friends speaks in chapter 8?
kids.option_a: Eliphaz
kids.option_b: Zophar
kids.option_c: Bildad
kids.option_d: Elihu
kids.explanation: Then answered Bildad the Shuhite (Job 8:1).

kids.question: What does Bildad say God will not do?
kids.option_a: He will not listen to Job's prayers
kids.option_b: He will not pervert judgment or right
kids.option_c: He will not restore Job's wealth
kids.option_d: He will not heal Job's sores
kids.explanation: Bildad says "Doth God pervert judgment? or doth the Almighty pervert justice?" (Job 8:3).

kids.question: What plant does Bildad use as an example of something that dies without water?
kids.option_a: An oak tree
kids.option_b: A vine in the desert
kids.option_c: A rush without mire or flag without water
kids.option_d: A flower in the field
kids.explanation: Bildad asks "Can the rush grow up without mire? can the flag grow without water?" as a picture of those who forget God (Job 8:11).

teens.question: What does Bildad say happened to Job's children and why?
teens.option_a: They were taken by God as a blessing
teens.option_b: If his children sinned, God cast them away for their transgression
teens.option_c: They perished because Job was not faithful enough
teens.option_d: They were taken to teach Job patience
teens.explanation: "If thy children have sinned against him, and he have cast them away for their transgression" (Job 8:4).

teens.question: What does Bildad promise Job if he is pure and upright?
teens.option_a: That God will immediately heal his boils
teens.option_b: That God will make the habitation of his righteousness prosperous
teens.option_c: That his three friends will confess their error
teens.option_d: That he will receive a vision explaining his suffering
teens.explanation: "If thou wert pure and upright; surely now he would awake for thee, and make the habitation of thy righteousness prosperous" (Job 8:6).

teens.question: Where does Bildad say his wisdom comes from?
teens.option_a: His own long experience
teens.option_b: The former age and the search of their fathers
teens.option_c: The wisdom of Solomon
teens.option_d: A dream from God
teens.explanation: "For enquire, I pray thee, of the former age, and prepare thyself to the search of their fathers" (Job 8:8).

teens.question: What does Bildad say the hypocrite's hope is like?
teens.option_a: A house built on sand
teens.option_b: A spider's house — a web that cannot stand
teens.option_c: A ship in the storm
teens.option_d: A candle in the wind
teens.explanation: "So are the paths of all that forget God; and the hypocrite's hope shall perish: Whose hope shall be cut off, and whose trust shall be a spider's web" (Job 8:13-14).

teens.question: What does Bildad say about Job's beginning compared to his latter end?
teens.option_a: His latter end will be worse than his beginning
teens.option_b: Though his beginning was small, his latter end should greatly increase
teens.option_c: His beginning and end will be the same
teens.option_d: His latter end is known only to God
teens.explanation: "Though thy beginning was small, yet thy latter end should greatly increase" (Job 8:7).

adults.question: What does Bildad's opening question "How long wilt thou speak these things?" reveal about his assessment of Job's lament?
adults.option_a: He is genuinely concerned for Job's health
adults.option_b: He dismisses Job's lament as a great wind — implying it is empty, prolonged, and spiritually dangerous
adults.option_c: He wants Job to speak more clearly
adults.option_d: He is asking Job to be more theologically precise
adults.explanation: "How long wilt thou speak these things? and how long shall the words of thy mouth be like a strong wind?" (Job 8:2) — Bildad calls Job's speeches empty as wind.

adults.question: What is the theological basis for Bildad's argument in Job 8:3?
adults.option_a: Divine omnipotence requires God to use suffering as a tool
adults.option_b: Divine justice is immutable — God cannot pervert judgment, therefore suffering must prove guilt
adults.option_c: God rewards only the richest and most prominent men
adults.option_d: Human wisdom is sufficient to judge divine actions
adults.explanation: "Doth God pervert judgment? or doth the Almighty pervert justice?" (Job 8:3) — Bildad builds his case on the absolute justice of God, which to him logically requires Job's guilt.

adults.question: What does Bildad imply about Job's children in Job 8:4 and how does this differ from what the reader knows from the prologue?
adults.option_a: He says they died innocently and God was wrong
adults.option_b: He suggests they died for their own sins — but the reader knows from chapters 1-2 that Job's integrity was tested, not punished
adults.option_c: He says their deaths were a divine mystery
adults.option_d: He blames Job for not praying enough for them
adults.explanation: "If thy children have sinned against him, and he have cast them away for their transgression" (Job 8:4) — the reader knows from ch. 1-2 that this is false.

adults.question: What does Bildad's appeal to "the former age" and the "fathers" in Job 8:8-10 reveal about his epistemology?
adults.option_a: He values personal spiritual experience above tradition
adults.option_b: He grounds authoritative knowledge in the accumulated wisdom of earlier generations rather than individual experience
adults.option_c: He believes the prophets hold all truth
adults.option_d: He relies on divine revelation given directly to him
adults.explanation: "Enquire...of the former age, and prepare thyself to the search of their fathers...shall not they teach thee, and tell thee?" (Job 8:8-10).

adults.question: What three images does Bildad use in Job 8:11-19 to describe the fate of those who forget God?
adults.option_a: Stars, rivers, and mountains
adults.option_b: Rush without water, spider's web, and a plant uprooted from stone
adults.option_c: Salt, wind, and dust
adults.option_d: Cloud, shadow, and night
adults.explanation: Bildad uses: (1) rush/flag dying without water (8:11-12); (2) the hypocrite's trust like a spider's web (8:14); (3) a plant whose roots wrap stone but is pulled up and the soil denies him (8:16-19).

adults.question: What does the phrase "leaning upon his house" in Job 8:15 suggest about the hypocrite's false security?
adults.option_a: The hypocrite has built a very strong house
adults.option_b: He shall lean upon his house but it shall not stand — the web and the house both crumble, showing false security collapses under weight
adults.option_c: He must renovate his house to be saved
adults.option_d: He is too lazy to build a real house
adults.explanation: "He shall lean upon his house, but it shall not stand: he shall hold it fast, but it shall not endure" (Job 8:15) — the spider's web cannot bear weight.

adults.question: What does Bildad mean by "the joy of his way" being destroyed in Job 8:19?
adults.option_a: The hypocrite was never truly joyful
adults.option_b: Behold, this is the joy of his way — ironically the plant's flourishing was its downfall, and out of the earth others shall grow — the wicked are replaced by others
adults.option_c: God removes joy as the first sign of punishment
adults.option_d: The wicked man's path leads to a joyless end
adults.explanation: "Behold, this is the joy of his way, and out of the earth shall others grow" (Job 8:19) — the hypocrite's prosperity is brief and soon replaced.

adults.question: What conditional promise does Bildad end with in Job 8:20-22, and what is its implicit accusation?
adults.option_a: God will forgive Job if he confesses openly
adults.option_b: God will not cast away a perfect man nor help evildoers — if Job were perfect he would be filled with laughing; since he suffers, he must be an evildoer
adults.option_c: Job must offer a hundred burnt offerings
adults.option_d: God will restore Job only after his death
adults.explanation: "Behold, God will not cast away a perfect man, neither will he help the evil doers" (Job 8:20) — the implication is that Job's suffering proves he is not perfect.

kids.question: Does Job think a man can be right before God in a legal argument?
kids.option_a: Yes, easily
kids.option_b: He wonders how a man can be just with God
kids.option_c: He says God always agrees with man
kids.option_d: He says he will argue his case before God
kids.explanation: Job says "But how should man be just with God?" (Job 9:2).

kids.question: What does Job say God does to great things in creation?
kids.option_a: God made them long ago and forgot them
kids.option_b: God doeth great things past finding out, marvellous things without number
kids.option_c: God shows them only to the righteous
kids.option_d: God destroys them when they grow too large
kids.explanation: Job says God "doeth great things past finding out; yea, and wonders without number" (Job 9:10).

kids.question: What does Job say he needs to face God in a legal court?
kids.option_a: A great sacrifice
kids.option_b: Someone to argue his case — a daysman or mediator
kids.option_c: A wiser friend than Eliphaz
kids.option_d: A dream or vision from God
kids.explanation: Job says "Neither is there any daysman betwixt us, that might lay his hand upon us both" (Job 9:33).

teens.question: What does Job say about trying to argue with God — how many of a thousand questions could a man answer?
teens.option_a: A thousand out of a thousand
teens.option_b: He could not answer one in a thousand
teens.option_c: Perhaps half of them
teens.option_d: Only the wisest could answer three
teens.explanation: "If he will contend with him, he cannot answer him one of a thousand" (Job 9:3).

teens.question: Name two constellations that Job attributes to God's creation in chapter 9.
teens.option_a: Orion and the Pleiades
teens.option_b: Arcturus and Orion
teens.option_c: Arcturus, Orion, and Pleiades
teens.option_d: The Bear and the Dragon
teens.explanation: "Which maketh Arcturus, Orion, and Pleiades, and the chambers of the south" (Job 9:9).

teens.question: What does Job say happens if God passes by and takes something?
teens.option_a: He will restore it later
teens.option_b: He passeth on, taketh away, and there is none that can hinder him or say What doest thou?
teens.option_c: He explains his reasons to the prophets
teens.option_d: He shows it to the angels first
teens.explanation: "If he pass through, or shut up, or gather together, then who can hinder him? Who will say unto him, What doest thou?" (Job 9:12).

teens.question: How does Job describe himself when he tries to speak to God?
teens.option_a: Bold and confident of his innocence
teens.option_b: Even if he were righteous his own mouth would condemn him
teens.option_c: Full of wise arguments that God must hear
teens.option_d: Silent and unable to form any words
teens.explanation: "If I justify myself, mine own mouth shall condemn me: if I say, I am perfect, it shall also prove me perverse" (Job 9:20).

teens.question: What does Job say in Job 9:33 that he lacks in his dispute with God?
teens.option_a: Enough sacrifices to offer
teens.option_b: A daysman — a mediator who could lay his hand upon them both
teens.option_c: A wise friend to speak for him
teens.option_d: The law written on stone
teens.explanation: "Neither is there any daysman betwixt us, that might lay his hand upon us both" (Job 9:33).

adults.question: What is the significance of Job's opening words "I know it is so of a truth" in Job 9:2?
adults.option_a: He is agreeing that he has sinned greatly
adults.option_b: He concedes Bildad's theological premise that God is just, but turns it to show the problem: no human can be just enough before such a God
adults.option_c: He is praising Bildad's speech as the best argument
adults.option_d: He is announcing that he will stop complaining
adults.explanation: "I know it is so of a truth: but how should man be just with God?" (Job 9:2) — Job accepts divine justice in principle but shows it creates an impossible situation for humans.

adults.question: What cosmic and geographical acts does Job list in Job 9:5-9 to describe God's overwhelming power?
adults.option_a: Feeding the poor and healing the sick
adults.option_b: Removing mountains, shaking the earth, darkening the sun, sealing up stars, treading on sea waves, and making Arcturus, Orion, and Pleiades
adults.option_c: Creating the rivers and setting the boundaries of nations
adults.option_d: Sending rain and making the seasons change
adults.explanation: Job lists: removing mountains (9:5), shaking earth (9:6), commanding the sun and sealing stars (9:7), walking on sea waves (9:8), making the constellations (9:9).

adults.question: What is the theological tension in Job's statement in Job 9:15: "Whom, though I were righteous, yet would I not answer, but I would make supplication to my judge"?
adults.option_a: Job believes he must earn the right to speak by more sacrifice
adults.option_b: Even if Job were righteous he could not effectively argue with God — the power asymmetry is so vast he can only plead, not argue
adults.option_c: Job admits his own guilt and asks for leniency
adults.option_d: Job is satisfied that God will hear his prayer
adults.explanation: "Whom, though I were righteous, yet would I not answer, but I would make supplication to my judge" (Job 9:15) — righteousness is insufficient leverage against God's overwhelming power.

adults.question: What does Job say about the earth in Job 9:24 that represents his most bitter accusation?
adults.option_a: The earth is cursed and shall pass away
adults.option_b: The earth is given into the hand of the wicked and God covereth the faces of its judges — if not he, who then is it?
adults.option_c: The earth belongs to God alone and cannot be contested
adults.option_d: The earth rewards the righteous with increase
adults.explanation: "The earth is given into the hand of the wicked: he covereth the faces of the judges thereof; if not, where and who is he?" (Job 9:24).

adults.question: How does Job use the metaphors of swift days — a post, swift ships, and an eagle — in Job 9:25-26?
adults.option_a: To praise God for the beauty of creation
adults.option_b: To lament the speed with which his brief, already joyless life is passing with no hope of recovery
adults.option_c: To urge his friends to act quickly on his behalf
adults.option_d: To describe the swiftness of God's judgment
adults.explanation: "Now my days are swifter than a post: they flee away...They are passed away as the swift ships: as the eagle that hasteth to the prey" (Job 9:25-26).

adults.question: What problem does Job raise in Job 9:28-31 about trying to make himself clean before God?
adults.option_a: He does not have access to water for ritual washing
adults.option_b: Even if he washed himself with snow water and made his hands ever so clean, God would plunge him in the ditch making his own clothes abhor him
adults.option_c: He needs a priest to declare him clean first
adults.option_d: He has no white garments to wear in God's presence
adults.explanation: "If I wash myself with snow water, and make my hands never so clean; Yet shalt thou plunge me in the ditch, and mine own clothes shall abhor me" (Job 9:30-31).

adults.question: What does Job mean in Job 9:32-33 when he says "He is not a man, as I am, that I should answer him"?
adults.option_a: God speaks a different language that Job cannot understand
adults.option_b: The total disparity between God and man makes legal contest impossible — hence the need for a mediator who shares both natures
adults.option_c: God has no interest in human legal proceedings
adults.option_d: Job is too weak physically to stand before God
adults.explanation: "He is not a man, as I am, that I should answer him, and we should come together in judgment. Neither is there any daysman betwixt us" (Job 9:32-33) — the need for a divine-human mediator.

adults.question: How does Job's concept of a "daysman" in Job 9:33 relate to the New Testament doctrine of Christ as mediator?
adults.option_a: It has no connection to New Testament theology
adults.option_b: Job's cry for a daysman who can lay his hand on both God and man anticipates the incarnation of Christ, the one mediator between God and men (1 Tim 2:5)
adults.option_c: The daysman is merely a legal concept with no spiritual meaning
adults.option_d: Job is asking for a human priest to represent him
adults.explanation: Job's longing for "any daysman betwixt us, that might lay his hand upon us both" (Job 9:33) is one of the most remarkable Messianic anticipations in the OT, fulfilled in Christ the God-man mediator.

kids.question: What does Job say his soul is weary of?
kids.option_a: Listening to his friends argue
kids.option_b: His life
kids.option_c: Sitting in the ashes
kids.option_d: Waiting for God to speak
kids.explanation: Job says "My soul is weary of my life; I will leave my complaint upon myself" (Job 10:1).

kids.question: What does Job say God made him out of?
kids.option_a: Stone and dust
kids.option_b: Clay
kids.option_c: Wood from the cedar tree
kids.option_d: Water from the deep
kids.explanation: Job says "Thou hast made me as the clay" (Job 10:9).

kids.question: What does Job ask God to remember about him?
kids.option_a: That he has offered many sacrifices
kids.option_b: That God made him like milk curdled into cheese — he is God's own creation
kids.option_c: That he has always prayed morning and evening
kids.option_d: That he is wiser than his three friends
kids.explanation: Job asks God to remember that "thou hast made me as the clay...Hast thou not poured me out as milk, and curdled me like cheese?" (Job 10:9, 10).

teens.question: What bold request does Job make of God at the start of chapter 10?
teens.option_a: That God would heal his boils immediately
teens.option_b: That God would tell him why he contends with him and show him wherefore he afflicts him
teens.option_c: That God would restore his children
teens.option_d: That God would silence his three friends
teens.explanation: "I will say unto God, Do not condemn me; shew me wherefore thou contendest with me" (Job 10:2).

teens.question: What rhetorical questions does Job ask to challenge the logic of God's treatment of him in Job 10:3-5?
teens.option_a: Dost thou delight in oppression? dost thou see as man sees?
teens.option_b: Is it good unto thee that thou shouldest oppress, that thou shouldest despise the work of thine hands...hast thou eyes of flesh?
teens.option_c: Why dost thou create if thou wilt only destroy?
teens.option_d: Art thou not the God who made me from the beginning?
teens.explanation: "Is it good unto thee that thou shouldest oppress, that thou shouldest despise the work of thine hands...Hast thou eyes of flesh? or seest thou as man seeth?" (Job 10:3-4).

teens.question: What does Job say God's hands did for him in Job 10:8?
teens.option_a: They wrote his name in the book of life
teens.option_b: They fashioned and made him together round about — yet now destroy him
teens.option_c: They shielded him from the Sabeans
teens.option_d: They placed him in the land of Uz
teens.explanation: "Thine hands have made me and fashioned me together round about; yet thou dost destroy me" (Job 10:8).

teens.question: How does Job describe God's knowledge of his innocence in Job 10:7?
teens.option_a: God does not know whether Job is innocent
teens.option_b: Thou knowest that I am not wicked, yet there is none that can deliver him out of God's hand
teens.option_c: God will discover Job's innocence eventually
teens.option_d: God knew Job was innocent from before his birth
teens.explanation: "Thou knowest that I am not wicked; and there is none that can deliver out of thine hand" (Job 10:7).

teens.question: What two contrasting things does Job say God gave him in Job 10:12?
teens.option_a: Sorrow and joy
teens.option_b: Life and favour, and visitation of his spirit preserved him
teens.option_c: Wealth and then poverty
teens.option_d: A family and then took them away
teens.explanation: "Thou hast granted me life and favour, and thy visitation hath preserved my spirit" (Job 10:12).

adults.question: What is the theological audacity of Job's speech in Job 10:1-2 where he refuses to restrain his complaint?
adults.option_a: Job is sinning in his bitterness and must repent
adults.option_b: Job models honest lament prayer — he speaks to God directly rather than about him, trusting that God can receive raw human anguish
adults.option_c: Job is losing his faith and must be corrected
adults.option_d: Job is performing a formal legal procedure
adults.explanation: "I will leave my complaint upon myself; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul. I will say unto God, Do not condemn me" (Job 10:1-2) — Job addresses God directly, a mark of authentic faith.

adults.question: What does Job mean in Job 10:3 when he asks whether it seems good to God to oppress and despise the work of his hands?
adults.option_a: Job thinks God is evil
adults.option_b: Job challenges God's internal consistency — a craftsman who takes pride in his work would not destroy it, so God's destruction of Job contradicts his own creative investment
adults.option_c: Job is trying to flatter God into releasing him
adults.option_d: Job is quoting the theology of the wicked nations
adults.explanation: "Is it good unto thee that thou shouldest oppress, that thou shouldest despise the work of thine hands?" (Job 10:3) — the creator-creature relationship becomes Job's argument.

adults.question: What does Job's embryological description in Job 10:10-11 reveal about his view of God as creator?
adults.option_a: God only created the soul, not the body
adults.option_b: God is intimately involved in forming human life from conception — pouring out as milk, curdling like cheese, clothing with skin and flesh, fencing with bones and sinews
adults.option_c: Life comes from the earth alone and God observes it
adults.option_d: The body is created by angels and the soul by God
adults.explanation: "Hast thou not poured me out as milk, and curdled me like cheese? Clothed me with skin and flesh, and fenced me with bones and sinews?" (Job 10:10-11).

adults.question: What dark reversal does Job describe in Job 10:13-17 between God's past care and present pursuit?
adults.option_a: God blessed him in youth but now ignores him
adults.option_b: The same God who formed and preserved him has hidden purposes to watch for his sin, not acquit him, and now hunts him like a lion
adults.option_c: God is testing him to make him stronger
adults.option_d: God has given his case to the angels to judge
adults.explanation: "And these things hast thou hid in thine heart: I know that this is with thee...If I be wicked, woe unto me; and if I be righteous, yet will I not lift up my head...Thou huntest me as a fierce lion" (Job 10:13-16).

adults.question: What does the phrase "witnesses against me" in Job 10:17 indicate about Job's legal metaphor?
adults.option_a: His friends are witnessing against him in a human court
adults.option_b: God produces fresh hosts of witnesses against Job, renewing his indignation, as if bringing new charges in a legal proceeding that never ends
adults.option_c: Job's past sins are now being revealed publicly
adults.option_d: The angels of God testify against Job before the heavenly court
adults.explanation: "Thou renewest thy witnesses against me, and increasest thine indignation upon me; changes and war are against me" (Job 10:17).

adults.question: What question does Job return to in Job 10:18 that echoes his lament in chapter 3?
adults.option_a: Why did God not listen to his prayers
adults.option_b: Wherefore then hast thou brought me forth out of the womb — why was he not simply carried from womb to grave without any existence
adults.option_c: Why did God take away his children first
adults.option_d: Why did God choose him for this test
adults.explanation: "Wherefore then hast thou brought me forth out of the womb? Oh that I had given up the ghost, and no eye had seen me!" (Job 10:18) — echoing the death-wish of ch. 3.

adults.question: What does Job request of God in Job 10:20 before death comes?
adults.option_a: That God would explain all the reasons for his suffering
adults.option_b: That God would cease from him for a little while so he can take comfort before he goes to the land of darkness
adults.option_c: That God would restore his family immediately
adults.option_d: That God would let his friends go free from blame
adults.explanation: "Are not my days few? cease then, and let me alone, that I may take comfort a little, Before I go whence I shall not return" (Job 10:20-21).

adults.question: How does Job describe the realm of the dead in Job 10:21-22, and what does this reveal about OT eschatology?
adults.option_a: A paradise where the righteous dwell with God in light
adults.option_b: A land of darkness, shadow of death, land of darkness as darkness itself, where the light is as darkness — a realm of complete and total absence
adults.option_c: A place of temporary waiting before resurrection
adults.option_d: A land ruled by angels who keep the dead in order
adults.explanation: "A land of darkness, as darkness itself; and of the shadow of death, without any order, and where the light is as darkness" (Job 10:22) — reflecting the OT Sheol as a shadowy, undifferentiated realm of the dead.

kids.question: Who gave the first speech in Job chapter 11?
kids.option_a: Zophar
kids.option_b: Eliphaz
kids.option_c: Bildad
kids.option_d: Elihu
kids.explanation: Zophar the Naamathite answered and spoke in Job chapter 11 (Job 11:1).

kids.question: What did Zophar say about God's wisdom?
kids.option_a: It is very high and cannot be fully known
kids.option_b: It is shared with all people
kids.option_c: It belongs only to angels
kids.option_d: It was given to Job
kids.explanation: Zophar declared that God's wisdom is higher than heaven and deeper than hell (Job 11:8).

kids.question: What did Zophar tell Job to do so that things would go well for him?
kids.option_a: Prepare his heart and stretch out his hands to God
kids.option_b: Build an altar and offer a sacrifice
kids.option_c: Go on a long journey
kids.option_d: Ask his friends for help
