kids.explanation: Zophar told Job, "If thou prepare thine heart, and stretch out thine hands toward him" things would go well (Job 11:13).

teens.question: What famous question does Zophar ask about searching out God in Job 11:7?
teens.option_a: Canst thou by searching find out God?
teens.option_b: Can a man be righteous before God?
teens.option_c: Who can number the stars?
teens.option_d: Where wast thou when I laid the foundations?
teens.explanation: Zophar asks, "Canst thou by searching find out God? canst thou find out the Almighty unto perfection?" (Job 11:7).

teens.question: How does Zophar describe the measure of God's wisdom in Job 11:9?
teens.option_a: Longer than the earth and broader than the sea
teens.option_b: Higher than the heavens and deeper than the ocean
teens.option_c: Wider than the desert and taller than the mountains
teens.option_d: Greater than all the stars combined
teens.explanation: The measure thereof is longer than the earth, and broader than the sea (Job 11:9).

teens.question: What does Zophar say God can perceive about a man in Job 11:11?
teens.option_a: Vain men and wickedness
teens.option_b: Only outward deeds
teens.option_c: A man's wealth and poverty
teens.option_d: A man's words alone
teens.explanation: He knoweth vain men: he seeth wickedness also; will he not then consider it? (Job 11:11).

teens.question: What image does Zophar use to describe a foolish man gaining understanding in Job 11:12?
teens.option_a: A wild ass's colt born a man
teens.option_b: A blind man seeing light
teens.option_c: A stone becoming water
teens.option_d: A dry tree bearing fruit
teens.explanation: For vain man would be wise, though man be born like a wild ass's colt (Job 11:12).

teens.question: What does Zophar promise Job if he repents in Job 11:17?
teens.option_a: His age shall be clearer than the noonday
teens.option_b: God will restore his wealth double
teens.option_c: His enemies will flee
teens.option_d: His children will return
teens.explanation: Thine age shall be clearer than the noonday; thou shalt shine forth, thou shalt be as the morning (Job 11:17).

adults.question: What charge does Zophar level against Job in Job 11:2-3?
adults.option_a: That a multitude of words should not go unanswered and Job's boasting should make men hold their peace
adults.option_b: That Job has blasphemed God openly
adults.option_c: That Job has sinned against his neighbors
adults.option_d: That Job has forsaken the law of Moses
adults.explanation: Should not the multitude of words be answered? and should a man full of talk be justified? (Job 11:2).

adults.question: What does Zophar wish God would do in Job 11:5-6?
adults.option_a: That God would speak and show Job the secrets of wisdom
adults.option_b: That God would restore Job's health
adults.option_c: That God would silence Job's friends
adults.option_d: That God would send rain to bless Job
adults.explanation: But oh that God would speak...and shew thee the secrets of wisdom, that they are double to that which is (Job 11:5-6).

adults.question: How does Zophar describe the dimensions of God's knowledge in Job 11:8-9?
adults.option_a: It is as high as heaven, deep as hell, longer than earth, broader than sea
adults.option_b: It is infinite in all directions beyond measure
adults.option_c: It fills the north and south with equal light
adults.option_d: It encompasses all time from creation to eternity
adults.explanation: It is as high as heaven...deeper than hell...The measure thereof is longer than the earth, and broader than the sea (Job 11:8-9).

adults.question: What action does Zophar say God can perform that no one can hinder in Job 11:10?
adults.option_a: If he cut off, and shut up, or gather together, then who can hinder him?
adults.option_b: If he sends famine no man can stop it
adults.option_c: If he speaks the mountains crumble
adults.option_d: If he commands the sea it obeys
adults.explanation: If he cut off, and shut up, or gather together, then who can hinder him? (Job 11:10).

adults.question: What specific acts of repentance does Zophar call Job to in Job 11:13-14?
adults.option_a: Prepare thine heart, stretch out hands, put iniquity away, and let not wickedness dwell in thy tabernacles
adults.option_b: Fast forty days and make a burnt offering
adults.option_c: Humble himself before his friends and confess his sin publicly
adults.option_d: Return to the land of his fathers and rebuild his altars
adults.explanation: If thou prepare thine heart, and stretch out thine hands toward him; If iniquity be in thine hand, put it far away (Job 11:13-14).

adults.question: What security does Zophar promise Job after repentance in Job 11:18-19?
adults.option_a: Thou shalt be secure because there is hope, and thou shalt take thy rest in safety with none to make thee afraid
adults.option_b: God will rebuild his house and restore his lands
adults.option_c: His enemies will become his servants
adults.option_d: He will receive a new name and a new covenant
adults.explanation: Thou shalt be secure, because there is hope; yea, thou shalt dig about thee, and thou shalt take thy rest in safety (Job 11:18-19).

adults.question: What fate does Zophar describe for the wicked in Job 11:20?
adults.option_a: The eyes of the wicked shall fail, and their hope shall be as the giving up of the ghost
adults.option_b: The wicked shall be swallowed by the earth
adults.option_c: Their children shall suffer for their sin
adults.option_d: They shall be driven into the wilderness
adults.explanation: But the eyes of the wicked shall fail, and they shall not escape, and their hope shall be as the giving up of the ghost (Job 11:20).

adults.question: Theologically, what is the core error of Zophar's argument in chapter 11?
adults.option_a: He assumes Job's suffering is direct proof of hidden sin, misapplying the doctrine of divine retribution
adults.option_b: He denies the resurrection of the dead
adults.option_c: He claims God cannot forgive sin
adults.option_d: He teaches that wisdom belongs only to the educated
adults.explanation: Zophar insists God exacts less than Job's iniquity deserves (Job 11:6), wrongly equating suffering with personal sin.

kids.question: What did Job say about himself compared to his friends in Job 12:3?
kids.option_a: I am not inferior to you
kids.option_b: I am wiser than all of you
kids.option_c: I have suffered more than you
kids.option_d: I have prayed longer than you
kids.explanation: Job declared, "But I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you" (Job 12:3).

kids.question: What did Job say even the animals and birds can teach in Job 12:7?
kids.option_a: That God made all things
kids.option_b: That God feeds the hungry
kids.option_c: That rain falls on the earth
kids.option_d: That the sun rises every day
kids.explanation: Job said, "Ask now the beasts, and they shall teach thee; and the fowls of the air, and they shall tell thee" (Job 12:7).

kids.question: Who does Job say holds the life of every creature in Job 12:10?
kids.option_a: God
kids.option_b: Angels
kids.option_c: Wise men
kids.option_d: Kings
kids.explanation: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the breath of all mankind (Job 12:10).

teens.question: How does Job describe himself mockingly in Job 12:4?
teens.option_a: As one who is mocked by his neighbour, a just, upright man
teens.option_b: As the weakest man in the land
teens.option_c: As a fool who spoke too boldly
teens.option_d: As a stranger in his own house
teens.explanation: I am as one mocked of his neighbour, who calleth upon God, and he answereth him: the just upright man is laughed to scorn (Job 12:4).

teens.question: What observation does Job make about the prosperous and the thief in Job 12:6?
teens.option_a: The tabernacles of robbers prosper and they that provoke God are secure
teens.option_b: The thief is always caught and punished
teens.option_c: Wealth comes only to the righteous
teens.option_d: God punishes the wicked without delay
teens.explanation: The tabernacles of robbers prosper, and they that provoke God are secure; into whose hand God bringeth abundantly (Job 12:6).

teens.question: What does Job say the ear does in Job 12:11?
teens.option_a: Trieth words as the mouth tasteth meat
teens.option_b: Hears only what the heart desires
teens.option_c: Listens for the voice of God
teens.option_d: Receives wisdom from the aged
teens.explanation: Doth not the ear try words? and the mouth taste his meat? (Job 12:11).

teens.question: What does Job say God does to counsellors in Job 12:17?
teens.option_a: He leadeth counsellors away spoiled
teens.option_b: He gives counsellors great wisdom
teens.option_c: He sends counsellors to comfort the afflicted
teens.option_d: He rewards counsellors with long life
teens.explanation: He leadeth counsellors away spoiled, and maketh the judges fools (Job 12:17).

teens.question: What does Job say God does to nations in Job 12:23?
teens.option_a: He increaseth the nations and destroyeth them
teens.option_b: He blesses the faithful nations forever
teens.option_c: He sets all nations at peace
teens.option_d: He divides the nations among the angels
teens.explanation: He increaseth the nations, and destroyeth them: he enlargeth the nations, and straiteneth them again (Job 12:23).

adults.question: How does Job characterize the wisdom his friends offer in Job 12:2?
adults.option_a: No doubt but ye are the people, and wisdom shall die with you
adults.option_b: Your wisdom is borrowed from your fathers
adults.option_c: You speak as men who have never suffered
adults.option_d: Your counsel is empty and without foundation
adults.explanation: No doubt but ye are the people, and wisdom shall die with you (Job 12:2).

adults.question: What does Job observe about the contempt shown to those who are righteous and call on God in Job 12:4-5?
adults.option_a: The just man who calls on God is mocked, while the prosperous despise the misfortune of others
adults.option_b: The righteous are honored above all others
adults.option_c: Those who pray are always vindicated quickly
adults.option_d: God silences those who mock the righteous
adults.explanation: The just upright man is laughed to scorn...He that is ready to slip with his feet is as a lamp despised in the thought of him that is at ease (Job 12:4-5).

adults.question: What four aspects of nature does Job cite in Job 12:7-9 as witnesses to God's sovereign work?
adults.option_a: Beasts, fowls of the air, the earth, and the fishes of the sea
adults.option_b: Mountains, rivers, stars, and clouds
adults.option_c: Wind, fire, rain, and earthquake
adults.option_d: Trees, grass, stones, and sand
adults.explanation: Ask the beasts, the fowls of the air, speak to the earth, and the fishes of the sea shall declare unto thee (Job 12:7-8).

adults.question: What theological principle does Job assert in Job 12:10 about all living things?
adults.option_a: In God's hand is the soul of every living thing and the breath of all mankind
adults.option_b: God gives life only to those who obey him
adults.option_c: All life returns to the dust by God's command
adults.option_d: The breath of life belongs to the angels
adults.explanation: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the breath of all mankind (Job 12:10).

adults.question: How does Job describe God's power over kings and judges in Job 12:17-18?
adults.option_a: He leadeth counsellors spoiled, makes judges fools, looses the bond of kings, and girds them with a girdle
adults.option_b: He crowns the humble and casts down the proud
adults.option_c: He establishes kingdoms for a thousand generations
adults.option_d: He gives wisdom only to those who fear him
adults.explanation: He leadeth counsellors away spoiled...He looseth the bond of kings, and girdeth their loins with a girdle (Job 12:17-18).

adults.question: What does Job say God does to the deep darkness in Job 12:22?
adults.option_a: He discovereth deep things out of darkness and bringeth out to light the shadow of death
adults.option_b: He fills the darkness with his glory
adults.option_c: He commands darkness to flee before the righteous
adults.option_d: He hides his mysteries in the deep
adults.explanation: He discovereth deep things out of darkness, and bringeth out to light the shadow of death (Job 12:22).

adults.question: How does Job describe what God does to the leaders of the earth in Job 12:24-25?
adults.option_a: He taketh away the heart of the chief people and causeth them to wander in a wilderness where there is no way, and they grope in darkness
adults.option_b: He rewards faithful leaders with long reigns
adults.option_c: He sends angels to guide every king
adults.option_d: He writes laws on the hearts of rulers
adults.explanation: He taketh away the heart of the chief people...and causeth them to wander in a wilderness where there is no way. They grope in the dark (Job 12:24-25).

adults.question: What is the rhetorical purpose of Job's appeal to creation's testimony in Job 12:7-9?
adults.option_a: To prove that even the beasts know God's sovereign hand is behind all things, undermining his friends' claim that suffering equals sin
adults.option_b: To show that Job studied natural philosophy
adults.option_c: To demonstrate that animals have greater wisdom than men
adults.option_d: To argue that God does not intervene in human affairs
adults.explanation: Job appeals to the beasts and earth as witnesses that "the hand of the LORD hath wrought this" (Job 12:9), asserting God's sovereignty over all including his own suffering.

kids.question: What did Job call his three friends in Job 13:4?
kids.option_a: Forgers of lies and physicians of no value
kids.option_b: Wise counsellors and good teachers
kids.option_c: Faithful friends and true helpers
kids.option_d: Messengers of comfort
kids.explanation: Job told his friends, "Ye are forgers of lies, ye are all physicians of no value" (Job 13:4).

kids.question: What bold thing did Job want to do before God in Job 13:3?
kids.option_a: Speak to the Almighty and reason with God
kids.option_b: Build an altar and offer a great sacrifice
kids.option_c: Sing praises to God all night
kids.option_d: Ask God to send an angel to help him
kids.explanation: Job said, "Surely I would speak to the Almighty, and I desire to reason with God" (Job 13:3).

kids.question: What famous statement of trust did Job make in Job 13:15?
kids.option_a: Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him
kids.option_b: God is my shepherd and I shall not want
kids.option_c: The Lord gave and the Lord hath taken away
kids.option_d: He restoreth my soul and leadeth me
kids.explanation: Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him: but I will maintain mine own ways before him (Job 13:15).

teens.question: What does Job say about the wisdom of his friends in Job 13:2?
teens.option_a: What ye know, the same do I know also; I am not inferior unto you
teens.option_b: Your wisdom is greater than mine but misapplied
teens.option_c: You know the ways of God but speak falsely
teens.option_d: You have forgotten the counsel of the aged
teens.explanation: What ye know, the same do I know also: I am not inferior unto you (Job 13:2).

teens.question: What does Job tell his friends would be their wisest action in Job 13:5?
teens.option_a: Hold your peace and it would be your wisdom
teens.option_b: Go and pray for three days in silence
teens.option_c: Return home and let Job speak to God alone
teens.option_d: Confess that they have spoken falsely
teens.explanation: O that ye would altogether hold your peace! and it should be your wisdom (Job 13:5).

teens.question: What does Job warn his friends about speaking falsely for God in Job 13:7-10?
teens.option_a: God will reprove them for accepting his person and speaking wickedly for him
teens.option_b: Their families will suffer for their lies
teens.option_c: They will lose all their possessions
teens.option_d: They will never see the face of God
teens.explanation: Will ye speak wickedly for God?...He will surely reprove you, if ye do secretly accept persons (Job 13:7, 10).

teens.question: What two things does Job ask God to withdraw in Job 13:21 so he can speak without terror?
teens.option_a: God's hand and God's terror
teens.option_b: God's wrath and God's silence
teens.option_c: God's judgement and God's distance
teens.option_d: God's fire and God's storm
teens.explanation: Withdraw thine hand far from me: and let not thy dread make me afraid (Job 13:21).

teens.question: What does Job ask God in Job 13:23?
teens.option_a: How many are mine iniquities and sins?
teens.option_b: Why hast thou forsaken me in my suffering?
teens.option_c: When will my suffering come to an end?
teens.option_d: What must I do to be restored to health?
teens.explanation: How many are mine iniquities and sins? make me to know my transgression and my sin (Job 13:23).

adults.question: How does Job describe his own observations in Job 13:1, distinguishing himself from his friends?
adults.option_a: Lo, mine eye hath seen all this, mine ear hath heard and understood it
adults.option_b: I have studied the scriptures more than any of you
adults.option_c: I have spoken with the elders and learned from their counsel
adults.option_d: My suffering has taught me what no book can give
adults.explanation: Lo, mine eye hath seen all this, mine ear hath heard and understood it (Job 13:1).

adults.question: What metaphor does Job use for his friends' counsel in Job 13:4?
adults.option_a: Ye are forgers of lies, ye are all physicians of no value
adults.option_b: Your words are like water poured on fire
adults.option_c: You are clouds without rain
adults.option_d: Your counsel is a broken reed
adults.explanation: But ye are forgers of lies, ye are all physicians of no value (Job 13:4).

adults.question: What does Job warn will happen to his friends if they show partiality to God's cause against him in Job 13:10?
adults.option_a: He will surely reprove you, if ye do secretly accept persons
adults.option_b: You will lose your place among the wise
adults.option_c: Your prayers will not be heard
adults.option_d: God will turn his face away from you
adults.explanation: He will surely reprove you, if ye do secretly accept persons (Job 13:10).

adults.question: What two images does Job use for the fate of his friends' defenses in Job 13:12?
adults.option_a: Your remembrances are like unto ashes, your bodies to bodies of clay
adults.option_b: Your arguments are like chaff and your wisdom like smoke
adults.option_c: Your words are like the wind and your counsel like dust
adults.option_d: Your confidence is as glass and your hope as morning dew
adults.explanation: Your remembrances are like unto ashes, your bodies to bodies of clay (Job 13:12).

adults.question: What does Job mean when he says in Job 13:15 that he will "maintain mine own ways before him"?
adults.option_a: He insists on presenting his case and defending his integrity before God even while trusting God
adults.option_b: He plans to live according to his own rules from now on
adults.option_c: He will not change his lifestyle regardless of God's will
adults.option_d: He will argue against God's commands
adults.explanation: Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him: but I will maintain mine own ways before him (Job 13:15) — Job trusts God yet insists on vindicating his integrity.

adults.question: What does Job identify as his basis for confidence that he shall be vindicated in Job 13:16?
adults.option_a: He also shall be my salvation: for an hypocrite shall not come before him
adults.option_b: His many years of righteous living
adults.option_c: The testimony of the angels
adults.option_d: His willingness to die for truth
adults.explanation: He also shall be my salvation: for an hypocrite shall not come before him (Job 13:16).

adults.question: What does Job ask God to reveal to him in Job 13:23-24?
adults.option_a: His iniquities and why God hides his face and counts him as an enemy
adults.option_b: The number of his remaining days
adults.option_c: The identity of the one who afflicted him
adults.option_d: Whether his children are in God's care
adults.explanation: How many are mine iniquities and sins?...Wherefore hidest thou thy face, and holdest me for thine enemy? (Job 13:23-24).

adults.question: What theological significance does Job 13:15 ("Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him") carry in the history of faith?
adults.option_a: It is one of Scripture's greatest expressions of unconditional trust in God amid suffering, anticipating New Testament faith that endures through death
adults.option_b: It proves that Job never truly doubted God
adults.option_c: It shows that Job had lost hope in any earthly restoration
adults.option_d: It is a statement of Job's willingness to become a martyr
adults.explanation: Job 13:15 is widely regarded as a pinnacle of Old Testament faith — trust that transcends even the threat of death itself.

kids.question: How does Job describe the life of a man born of a woman in Job 14:1?
kids.option_a: Few of days and full of trouble
kids.option_b: Strong and full of hope
kids.option_c: Short but filled with joy
kids.option_d: Long but ending in sorrow
kids.explanation: Man that is born of a woman is of few days, and full of trouble (Job 14:1).

kids.question: What does Job say about a tree that is cut down in Job 14:7?
kids.option_a: There is hope for a tree; it will sprout again
kids.option_b: A cut tree is gone forever
kids.option_c: The tree's roots hold all its life
kids.option_d: The tree must be watered to survive
kids.explanation: For there is hope of a tree, if it be cut down, that it will sprout again (Job 14:7).

kids.question: What great question does Job ask about death in Job 14:14?
kids.option_a: If a man die, shall he live again?
kids.option_b: Why do the righteous die young?
kids.option_c: Will God remember the dead?
kids.option_d: Where does the soul go after death?
kids.explanation: If a man die, shall he live again? all the days of my appointed time will I wait (Job 14:14).

teens.question: To what two things does Job compare the brevity of human life in Job 14:2?
teens.option_a: A flower that cometh forth and is cut down, and a shadow that fleeth
teens.option_b: A candle that burns quickly and water that dries up
teens.option_c: A morning cloud and the early dew that passes away
teens.option_d: A river that runs fast and a wind that blows once
teens.explanation: He cometh forth like a flower, and is cut down: he fleeth also as a shadow, and continueth not (Job 14:2).

teens.question: What limitation does Job ask God to set in Job 14:5-6?
teens.option_a: That God would look away and let man enjoy his day like a hireling
teens.option_b: That God would shorten the days of suffering
teens.option_c: That God would grant one final prayer before death
teens.option_d: That God would reveal his purpose to Job
teens.explanation: Seeing his days are determined...turn from him, that he may rest, till he shall accomplish, as an hireling, his day (Job 14:5-6).

teens.question: How does Job contrast the fate of a man with that of a tree in Job 14:7-10?
teens.option_a: A tree has hope of sprouting again, but a man giveth up the ghost and wasteth away
teens.option_b: A tree provides shade but a man seeks shelter
teens.option_c: A tree grows taller each year but a man grows weaker
teens.option_d: A tree is rooted in earth but a man walks above it
teens.explanation: There is hope of a tree...but man dieth, and wasteth away: yea, man giveth up the ghost, and where is he? (Job 14:7, 10).

teens.question: What does Job say about rivers and floods in connection with death in Job 14:11?
teens.option_a: As the waters fail from the sea, and the flood decayeth and drieth up: so man lieth down, and riseth not
teens.option_b: The flood comes and goes but man endures forever
teens.option_c: The river flows to the sea but man returns to the dust
teens.option_d: Waters rise and fall but God's word remains
teens.explanation: As the waters fail from the sea, and the flood decayeth and drieth up: So man lieth down, and riseth not (Job 14:11).

teens.question: What does Job hope God will do for him if he waits through his appointed time in Job 14:15?
teens.option_a: God will call and Job will answer, and God will have a desire to the work of his hands
teens.option_b: God will restore his health and rebuild his house
teens.option_c: God will send an angel with a message of comfort
teens.option_d: God will reveal the reason for all his suffering
teens.explanation: Thou shalt call, and I will answer thee: thou wilt have a desire to the work of thine hands (Job 14:15).

adults.question: What does Job say about the cleanness of the unclean in Job 14:4?
adults.option_a: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? not one
adults.option_b: The unclean can be made clean by water and sacrifice
adults.option_c: Only God can make the sinful pure
adults.option_d: No man is born without the stain of sin
adults.explanation: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? not one (Job 14:4).

adults.question: What specific limits does Job say God has set for man in Job 14:5?
adults.option_a: His days are determined, the number of his months are with thee, thou hast appointed his bounds that he cannot pass
adults.option_b: God has set the length of a man's suffering
adults.option_c: God has numbered a man's words before he speaks
adults.option_d: God has appointed when each man shall repent
adults.explanation: Seeing his days are determined, the number of his months are with thee, thou hast appointed his bounds that he cannot pass (Job 14:5).

adults.question: How does Job describe the revival of a tree cut down in Job 14:7-9?
adults.option_a: Through the scent of water it will bud and bring forth boughs like a plant
adults.option_b: The tree's roots send up new life in spring
adults.option_c: Its stump remains green beneath the snow
adults.option_d: New shoots spring forth from seeds it dropped
adults.explanation: Though the root thereof wax old in the earth...yet through the scent of water it will bud, and bring forth boughs like a plant (Job 14:8-9).

adults.question: What does Job say about the permanence of a man's absence in death in Job 14:12?
adults.option_a: Man lieth down and riseth not: till the heavens be no more, they shall not awake, nor be raised out of their sleep
adults.option_b: A man's soul lingers near his grave for many days
adults.option_c: The dead return to dust and are forgotten
adults.option_d: Death takes man but God can restore
adults.explanation: Man lieth down, and riseth not: till the heavens be no more, they shall not awake, nor be raised out of their sleep (Job 14:12).

adults.question: What does Job ask God to do in Job 14:13 regarding Sheol?
adults.option_a: That God would hide him in Sheol, keep him secret, and appoint him a set time and remember him
adults.option_b: That God would allow him to speak to the dead
adults.option_c: That God would bring him quickly to Sheol to end his pain
adults.option_d: That God would reveal what happens in Sheol
adults.explanation: O that thou wouldest hide me in the grave, that thou wouldest keep me secret...that thou wouldest appoint me a set time, and remember me! (Job 14:13).

adults.question: What does Job say God seals up in Job 14:17 as part of his judgment?
adults.option_a: My transgression is sealed up in a bag, and thou sewest up mine iniquity
adults.option_b: My prayers are sealed before the throne
adults.option_c: My days are counted and stored up in heaven
adults.option_d: My words are recorded and cannot be erased
adults.explanation: My transgression is sealed up in a bag, and thou sewest up mine iniquity (Job 14:17).

adults.question: How does Job describe the erosion of human hope in Job 14:18-19?
adults.option_a: As mountains fall and rocks are moved, and waters wear stones and washings wash away the dust of the earth, so thou destroyest the hope of man
adults.option_b: Hope fades like morning mist when trouble comes
adults.option_c: The hope of man is like a fragile vessel
adults.option_d: Man's hope is eaten away by his own doubts
adults.explanation: The mountain falling cometh to nought...The waters wear the stones...so thou destroyest the hope of man (Job 14:18-19).

adults.question: How does Job 14:14's question "If a man die, shall he live again?" function within the larger argument of the book?
adults.option_a: It is the first great resurrection question in the OT poetic books, expressing hope for vindication beyond death even as Job doubts its possibility in this life
adults.option_b: It proves Job did not believe in any afterlife
adults.option_c: It is a rhetorical device to blame God for not revealing the afterlife
adults.option_d: It anticipates only the physical restoration Job later receives
adults.explanation: Job 14:14 is a pivotal OT resurrection question; Job expresses guarded hope: "all the days of my appointed time will I wait, till my change come" (Job 14:14).

kids.question: Who speaks in Job chapter 15?
kids.option_a: Eliphaz the Temanite
kids.option_b: Bildad the Shuhite
kids.option_c: Zophar the Naamathite
kids.option_d: Elihu the Buzite
kids.explanation: Then answered Eliphaz the Temanite (Job 15:1) — this is Eliphaz's second speech.

kids.question: What does Eliphaz say Job's own mouth does in Job 15:6?
kids.option_a: Thine own mouth condemneth thee
kids.option_b: Your words prove your righteousness
kids.option_c: Your lips speak only truth
kids.option_d: Your prayers go unanswered
kids.explanation: Thine own mouth condemneth thee, and not I: yea, thine own lips testify against thee (Job 15:6).

kids.question: What does Eliphaz say the wicked man is filled with in Job 15:20?
kids.option_a: The wicked man travaileth with pain all his days
kids.option_b: Great riches but no peace
kids.option_c: Fear of God and trembling
kids.option_d: The wisdom of his fathers
kids.explanation: The wicked man travaileth with pain all his days, and the number of years is hidden to the oppressor (Job 15:20).

teens.question: What rhetorical question does Eliphaz ask Job in Job 15:7-8?
teens.option_a: Art thou the first man that was born? or wast thou made before the hills? Hast thou heard the secret of God?
teens.option_b: Were you present at creation and do you know the depths of wisdom?
teens.option_c: Have you counted the stars and measured the deep?
teens.option_d: Did God speak to you in a dream or a vision?
teens.explanation: Art thou the first man that was born? or wast thou made before the hills? Hast thou heard the secret of God? (Job 15:7-8).

teens.question: What does Eliphaz say Job is doing to fear and religion in Job 15:4?
teens.option_a: Thou castest off fear, and restrainest prayer before God
teens.option_b: You are teaching others to doubt God
teens.option_c: You are making the young men rebellious
teens.option_d: You are using prayer as a weapon against God
teens.explanation: Yea, thou castest off fear, and restrainest prayer before God (Job 15:4).

teens.question: What does Eliphaz say about the purity of the heavens and angels in Job 15:15?
teens.option_a: He putteth no trust in his saints; yea, the heavens are not clean in his sight
teens.option_b: The angels sing of his holiness forever
teens.option_c: The heavens declare his righteousness
teens.option_d: His servants minister before him in perfect holiness
teens.explanation: Behold, he putteth no trust in his saints; yea, the heavens are not clean in his sight (Job 15:15).

teens.question: How does Eliphaz describe what the wicked man hears in Job 15:21?
teens.option_a: A dreadful sound is in his ears; in prosperity the destroyer shall come upon him
teens.option_b: The voice of God calling him to repent
teens.option_c: The cries of those he has wronged
teens.option_d: Thunder and storms pursuing him
teens.explanation: A dreadful sound is in his ears: in prosperity the destroyer shall come upon him (Job 15:21).

teens.question: What does Eliphaz say about the wicked man's dwelling in Job 15:28?
teens.option_a: He dwelleth in desolate cities, in houses no man inhabiteth
teens.option_b: His house is built on sand and falls in the storm
teens.option_c: The wicked man's tent is torn from its pegs
teens.option_d: His home becomes a dwelling for wild beasts
teens.explanation: And he dwelleth in desolate cities, and in houses which no man inhabiteth, which are ready to become heaps (Job 15:28).

adults.question: How does Eliphaz characterize Job's speech in Job 15:2-3?
adults.option_a: Should a wise man utter vain knowledge and fill his belly with the east wind, and reason with unprofitable talk?
adults.option_b: Job's words are foolish because he speaks without suffering
adults.option_c: Your speech brings dishonor upon your family
adults.option_d: A wise man does not defend himself before God
adults.explanation: Should a wise man utter vain knowledge, and fill his belly with the east wind?...with speeches wherewith he can do no good? (Job 15:2-3).

adults.question: What claim about exclusive wisdom does Eliphaz challenge in Job 15:9-10?
adults.option_a: What knowest thou that we know not? with us are both the grayheaded and very aged men, much elder than thy father
adults.option_b: You speak as though God has given you alone his counsel
adults.option_c: Your wisdom came from foreign lands, not from our traditions
adults.option_d: Job thinks his affliction has made him wiser than any living man
adults.explanation: What knowest thou, that we know not?...with us are both the grayheaded and very aged men, much elder than thy father (Job 15:9-10).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz say about man's condition in Job 15:14?
adults.option_a: What is man, that he should be clean? and he which is born of a woman, that he should be righteous?
adults.option_b: Man cannot argue with God no matter how righteous he appears
adults.option_c: Man born of dust can never approach the holy God
adults.option_d: No flesh can stand before the judgment of the Almighty
adults.explanation: What is man, that he should be clean? and he which is born of a woman, that he should be righteous? (Job 15:14).

adults.question: How does Eliphaz describe man's moral condition in Job 15:16?
adults.option_a: How much more abominable and filthy is man, which drinketh iniquity like water
adults.option_b: Man is born corrupt and can do no good of himself
adults.option_c: Every thought of man's heart is evil from his youth
adults.option_d: Man's sin is as deep as the sea and as high as the heavens
adults.explanation: How much more abominable and filthy is man, which drinketh iniquity like water? (Job 15:16).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz say about the wicked man's wealth in Job 15:29?
adults.option_a: He shall not be rich, neither shall his substance continue, neither shall he prolong the perfection thereof upon the earth
adults.option_b: The wicked man gathers wealth but it is stolen from him
adults.option_c: Riches gained by wickedness vanish in a single night
adults.option_d: The wicked man's gold turns to dust in his hands
adults.explanation: He shall not be rich, neither shall his substance continue, neither shall he prolong the perfection thereof upon the earth (Job 15:29).

adults.question: What agricultural image does Eliphaz use to describe the wicked in Job 15:30-33?
adults.option_a: He shall not depart out of darkness; his branch shall not be green; and he shall cast off his flower as the vine, and as the olive
adults.option_b: The wicked man is like a weed among wheat
adults.option_c: Like a vine without water that withers before harvest
adults.option_d: The wicked man's tree is struck by lightning
adults.explanation: He shall not depart out of darkness...his branch shall not be green...he shall cast off his flower as the vine (Job 15:30, 32-33).

adults.question: What does Eliphaz say the wicked man's congregation shall be in Job 15:34?
adults.option_a: The congregation of hypocrites shall be desolate, and fire shall consume the tabernacles of bribery
adults.option_b: The assembly of the wicked shall scatter before the righteous
adults.option_c: Those who gather with the wicked shall perish with them
adults.option_d: The wicked man's followers shall be put to shame
adults.explanation: For the congregation of hypocrites shall be desolate, and fire shall consume the tabernacles of bribery (Job 15:34).

adults.question: What is the theological weakness in Eliphaz's second speech in chapter 15 compared to his first speech in chapter 4?
adults.option_a: He has moved from gentle rebuke to personal attack, accusing Job of undermining religion and filling himself with wind, without evidence beyond Job's protest of innocence
adults.option_b: He no longer believes in God's mercy
adults.option_c: He has abandoned the doctrine of repentance
adults.option_d: He blames Job's children for their father's trouble
adults.explanation: Eliphaz's second speech is harsher — he accuses Job of casting off fear and restraining prayer (Job 15:4) with no new evidence, revealing his argument has hardened into personal attack.

kids.question: What did Job call his three friends in Job 16:2?
kids.option_a: Miserable comforters
kids.option_b: Faithful companions
kids.option_c: Wise counsellors
kids.option_d: Unkind strangers
kids.explanation: Job said, "I have heard many such things: miserable comforters are ye all" (Job 16:2).

kids.question: Where does Job say his witness is in Job 16:19?
kids.option_a: In heaven
kids.option_b: In the temple
kids.option_c: In his heart
kids.option_d: Among the angels
kids.explanation: Also now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and my record is on high (Job 16:19).

kids.question: What does Job say his eyes do because of his suffering in Job 16:20?
kids.option_a: Mine eye poureth out tears unto God
kids.option_b: My eyes grow dim with grief
kids.option_c: I cannot see through my tears
kids.option_d: My eyes close and will not open
kids.explanation: My friends scorn me: but mine eye poureth out tears unto God (Job 16:20).

teens.question: What does Job say he would do if his friends were in his place in Job 16:4-5?
teens.option_a: I could heap up words against you, but I would strengthen you with my mouth and comfort you
teens.option_b: I would sit silently and weep with you
teens.option_c: I would offer a sacrifice on your behalf
teens.option_d: I would not accuse you as you have accused me
teens.explanation: I could heap up words against you, and shake mine head at you. But I would strengthen you with my mouth (Job 16:4-5).

teens.question: How does Job describe God's attack on him in Job 16:9?
teens.option_a: He teareth me in his wrath, who hateth me: he gnasheth upon me with his teeth
teens.option_b: God has forsaken me and left me without shelter
teens.option_c: God sends his arrows against me without cause
teens.option_d: God has sealed my mouth so I cannot speak
teens.explanation: He teareth me in his wrath, who hateth me: he gnasheth upon me with his teeth; mine enemy sharpeneth his eyes upon me (Job 16:9).

teens.question: What physical sufferings does Job describe in Job 16:15-16?
teens.option_a: He has sewed sackcloth on his skin, defiled his horn in the dust, his face is foul with weeping
teens.option_b: Covered in wounds from head to foot with no comfort
teens.option_c: His bones ache and his flesh wastes away before God
teens.option_d: He is unable to eat or drink and grows weaker each day
teens.explanation: I have sewed sackcloth upon my skin, and defiled my horn in the dust. My face is foul with weeping (Job 16:15-16).

teens.question: What does Job say about the purity of his prayer in Job 16:17?
teens.option_a: Not for any injustice in mine hands: also my prayer is pure
teens.option_b: Though I suffer, I have not sinned against the poor
teens.option_c: My hands are clean even if my body is broken
teens.option_d: God knows I never lifted my hand against the innocent
teens.explanation: Not for any injustice in mine hands: also my prayer is pure (Job 16:17).

teens.question: What does Job ask the earth not to do in Job 16:18?
teens.option_a: Cover not thou my blood, and let my cry have no place
teens.option_b: Let not the dust receive my bones before I am vindicated
teens.option_c: Do not swallow me as thou hast swallowed others
teens.option_d: Receive not my tears as if they were the waters of the sea
teens.explanation: O earth, cover not thou my blood, and let my cry have no place (Job 16:18).

adults.question: What question does Job pose to his friends in Job 16:3 about their speeches?
adults.option_a: Shall vain words have an end? or what emboldeneth thee that thou answerest?
adults.option_b: How long will you torment me with your words?
adults.option_c: Do you think God is pleased with your defence of him?
adults.option_d: Will you speak falsely for God and think you do him honour?
adults.explanation: Shall vain words have an end? or what emboldeneth thee that thou answerest? (Job 16:3).

adults.question: How does Job describe God handing him over to the ungodly in Job 16:11?
adults.option_a: God hath delivered me to the ungodly, and turned me over into the hands of the wicked
adults.option_b: God has allowed my enemies to surround my tent
adults.option_c: The Almighty has sent my adversaries against me
adults.option_d: My tormentors were appointed by the heavenly council
adults.explanation: God hath delivered me to the ungodly, and turned me over into the hands of the wicked (Job 16:11).

adults.question: What military imagery does Job use to describe God's attack in Job 16:12-14?
adults.option_a: He hath set me up for his mark, his archers surround me, he breaketh me with breach upon breach like a mighty man
adults.option_b: God has laid siege to my city and broken down my walls
adults.option_c: God's sword has pierced me through and through
adults.option_d: Like a great flood God's judgment overwhelms me
adults.explanation: He hath set me up for his mark...His archers compass me round about...he breaketh me with breach upon breach, he runneth upon me like a giant (Job 16:12-14).

adults.question: What does Job call his advocate in heaven in Job 16:19-21?
adults.option_a: My witness in heaven and my record on high who shall plead for a man with God as a man pleadeth for his neighbour
adults.option_b: The holy one who sits at God's right hand
adults.option_c: An angel of mercy appointed to my cause
adults.option_d: The spirit of my righteousness before the throne
adults.explanation: My witness is in heaven...my record is on high...Also my friends scorn me...O that one might plead for a man with God, as a man pleadeth for his neighbour (Job 16:19-21).

adults.question: What does Job say about his spirit in Job 16:22?
adults.option_a: When a few years are come, then I shall go the way whence I shall not return
adults.option_b: My spirit grows weaker with each passing day
adults.option_c: The spirit God gave me will outlast my suffering
adults.option_d: I will not yield up my spirit until I am vindicated
adults.explanation: When a few years are come, then I shall go the way whence I shall not return (Job 16:22).

adults.question: What does the concept of a heavenly witness/advocate in Job 16:19-21 anticipate theologically?
adults.option_a: It prefigures the intercessory work of Christ as the mediator between God and man
adults.option_b: It establishes the Jewish doctrine of guardian angels
adults.option_c: It proves that the dead can hear the prayers of the living
adults.option_d: It introduces the idea that all righteous men become advocates after death
adults.explanation: Job's cry for a heavenly witness who would plead his cause (Job 16:19-21) anticipates the NT teaching of Christ as mediator and advocate (1 Timothy 2:5; 1 John 2:1).

adults.question: What is the significance of Job asking the earth not to cover his blood in Job 16:18?
adults.option_a: Uncovered blood cried out for justice in ancient Near Eastern thought (cf. Genesis 4:10), so Job wants his innocent suffering to remain as a testimony before God demanding vindication
adults.option_b: Job feared his death would be forgotten by his descendants
adults.option_c: He wanted his grave to remain visible as a monument
adults.option_d: Blood on the ground was considered a curse in Israelite culture
adults.explanation: O earth, cover not thou my blood (Job 16:18) — as Abel's blood cried from the ground, Job wants his innocent suffering to cry out before God (cf. Genesis 4:10).

adults.question: How does Job's self-description as one whose face is foul with weeping (Job 16:16) connect to his claim of pure prayer in Job 16:17?
adults.option_a: The contrast shows Job's outward agony is matched by inward integrity — his weeping is not guilt but grief, and his prayer is uncorrupted by injustice
adults.option_b: Job weeps in repentance even as he protests his innocence
adults.option_c: His tears demonstrate that he has no anger toward God
adults.option_d: The foul face is a sign of his complete humility before the Almighty
adults.explanation: My face is foul with weeping...not for any injustice in mine hands: also my prayer is pure (Job 16:16-17) — outward distress and inward purity stand together in Job's testimony.

kids.question: What does Job say about his days in Job 17:1?
kids.option_a: My days are past
kids.option_b: My days are numbered by God
kids.option_c: My days are full of sorrow
kids.option_d: My days will be restored
kids.explanation: My breath is corrupt, my days are extinct, the graves are ready for me (Job 17:1).

kids.question: Who does Job say has made him a byword in Job 17:6?
kids.option_a: God hath made me a byword of the people
kids.option_b: His enemies in the marketplace
kids.option_c: The rulers of his city
kids.option_d: His own children
kids.explanation: He hath made me also a byword of the people; and aforetime I was as a tabret (Job 17:6).

kids.question: Where does Job say his hope has gone in Job 17:15?
kids.option_a: Where is now my hope?
kids.option_b: My hope was carried away by the wind
kids.option_c: Hope has been taken by my enemies
kids.option_d: My hope is hidden in the grave
kids.explanation: And where is now my hope? as for my hope, who shall see it? (Job 17:15).

teens.question: What does Job ask God to do in Job 17:3 regarding his pledge?
teens.option_a: Lay down now, put me in a surety with thee; who is he that will strike hands with me?
teens.option_b: Give me one more day to present my case
teens.option_c: Send me a messenger from heaven
teens.option_d: Record my words before I die
teens.explanation: Lay down now, put me in a surety with thee; who is he that will strike hands with me? (Job 17:3).

teens.question: What does Job observe about the fate of the upright in Job 17:8-9?
teens.option_a: The upright man is astonished, the innocent stirs up against the hypocrite, and the righteous holdeth on his way and gains strength
teens.option_b: The upright man is rewarded swiftly by God
teens.option_c: The righteous man is protected from all suffering
teens.option_d: God reveals his purposes to the upright in every generation
teens.explanation: Upright men shall be astonied at this...the innocent shall stir up himself against the hypocrite. The righteous also shall hold on his way, and he that hath clean hands shall be stronger and stronger (Job 17:8-9).

teens.question: What does Job say about the wisdom of his friends in Job 17:10?
teens.option_a: Return, and come now: for I cannot find one wise man among you
teens.option_b: Your counsel is empty and your comfort false
teens.option_c: You speak as men who have never known affliction
teens.option_d: Your wisdom ends where my suffering begins
teens.explanation: But as for you all, do ye return, and come now: for I cannot find one wise man among you (Job 17:10).

teens.question: What does Job say about his plans and the thoughts of his heart in Job 17:11?
teens.option_a: My days are past, my purposes are broken off, even the thoughts of my heart
teens.option_b: My plans were great but God has shattered them
teens.option_c: Every purpose I formed has turned to grief
teens.option_d: My thoughts are only of death and the grave
teens.explanation: My days are past, my purposes are broken off, even the thoughts of my heart (Job 17:11).

teens.question: What does Job say about the location of his rest and hope in Job 17:13-16?
teens.option_a: If I wait, the grave is mine house; my hope shall go down to the bars of the pit, and rest together in the dust
teens.option_b: He will wait for God in the depths of his grief
teens.option_c: His rest comes only when the suffering ends
teens.option_d: Hope will return when the morning comes
teens.explanation: If I wait, the grave is mine house: I have made my bed in the darkness...They shall go down to the bars of the pit, when our rest together is in the dust (Job 17:13, 16).

adults.question: What physical description does Job give of his condition in Job 17:1?
adults.option_a: My breath is corrupt, my days are extinct, the graves are ready for me
adults.option_b: My body is consumed by worms and my strength is dried up
adults.option_c: My skin is broken and my flesh cleaves to my bones
adults.option_d: My eyes are blind and my ears no longer hear
adults.explanation: My breath is corrupt, my days are extinct, the graves are ready for me (Job 17:1).

adults.question: What does Job say about God's action toward his friends' understanding in Job 17:4?
adults.option_a: Thou hast hid their heart from understanding: therefore shalt thou not exalt them
adults.option_b: God has allowed them to be deceived by their own wisdom
adults.option_c: Their hearts are hardened because they have not suffered
adults.option_d: God will judge them for their false comfort
adults.explanation: For thou hast hid their heart from understanding: therefore shalt thou not exalt them (Job 17:4).

adults.question: What physical deterioration does Job describe in Job 17:7?
adults.option_a: Mine eye also is dim by reason of sorrow, and all my members are as a shadow
adults.option_b: My limbs fail me and my flesh rots before my eyes
adults.option_c: My hands are too weak to lift and my feet refuse to walk
adults.option_d: My face is pale and my skin hangs from my bones
adults.explanation: Mine eye also is dim by reason of sorrow, and all my members are as a shadow (Job 17:7).

adults.question: How does Job describe the changing of night and day by his friends in Job 17:12?
adults.option_a: They change the night into day: the light is short because of darkness
adults.option_b: They call my darkness light and my sorrow joy
adults.option_c: They see only sunshine while I live in shadow
adults.option_d: My friends count the days of my suffering as days of blessing
adults.explanation: They change the night into day: the light is short because of darkness (Job 17:12).

adults.question: What is the significance of Job saying "the righteous shall hold on his way" in Job 17:9?
adults.option_a: Even surrounded by accusation and despair, Job affirms that the person with clean hands grows stronger — a declaration of persevering faith
adults.option_b: He is predicting his own recovery
adults.option_c: He teaches that only perfect people can face God
adults.option_d: He is arguing that the righteous never suffer
adults.explanation: The righteous also shall hold on his way, and he that hath clean hands shall be stronger and stronger (Job 17:9) — faith perseveres even in the pit.

adults.question: What does Job say to his friends about their attempts to renew their arguments in Job 17:10?
adults.option_a: But as for you all, do ye return, and come now: for I cannot find one wise man among you
adults.option_b: I have heard enough — your counsel only deepens my wounds
adults.option_c: Speak no more to me of God's justice, for you do not understand it
adults.option_d: Your words are as empty as the wind that passes over the desert
adults.explanation: But as for you all, do ye return, and come now: for I cannot find one wise man among you (Job 17:10).

adults.question: How does Job describe the corruption of the human community around him in Job 17:5?
adults.option_a: He that speaketh flattery to his friends, even the eyes of his children shall fail
adults.option_b: My companions have turned against me to gain favour with the powerful
adults.option_c: Those who mocked me will themselves be mocked
adults.option_d: My name has been defamed among those who once called me friend
adults.explanation: He that speaketh flattery to his friends, even the eyes of his children shall fail (Job 17:5).

adults.question: What is the theological purpose of chapter 17 in the structure of Job's speeches?
adults.option_a: It represents the nadir of Job's despair — his hope seems extinguished and the grave appears to be his only destination, yet he still addresses God directly, showing faith survives even the darkest night
adults.option_b: It marks Job's final rejection of God's sovereignty
adults.option_c: It proves that Job has abandoned belief in an afterlife
adults.option_d: It sets up Elihu's entrance by showing that the three friends have completely failed
adults.explanation: Chapter 17 is Job's darkest soliloquy; hope seems buried with him (Job 17:15-16), yet his direct address to God throughout shows that even in despair he has not abandoned relationship with God.

kids.question: Who speaks in Job chapter 18?
kids.option_a: Bildad the Shuhite
kids.option_b: Eliphaz the Temanite
kids.option_c: Zophar the Naamathite
kids.option_d: Elihu the Buzite
kids.explanation: Then answered Bildad the Shuhite (Job 18:1) — this is Bildad's second speech.

kids.question: What does Bildad say happens to the light of the wicked in Job 18:5?
kids.option_a: The light of the wicked shall be put out
kids.option_b: The wicked man's light burns fiercely for a time
kids.option_c: His lamp goes dark only when he repents
kids.option_d: The flame of the wicked lights up the whole earth
kids.explanation: Yea, the light of the wicked shall be put out, and the spark of his fire shall not shine (Job 18:5).

kids.question: What does Bildad say will happen to the wicked man's name in Job 18:17?
kids.option_a: His remembrance shall perish from the earth
kids.option_b: His name will be remembered as a warning
kids.option_c: His children will carry his name forever
kids.option_d: His name will be written in the book of the wicked
kids.explanation: His remembrance shall perish from the earth, and he shall have no name in the street (Job 18:17).

teens.question: What does Bildad accuse Job of in Job 18:3?
teens.option_a: Wherefore are we counted as beasts, and reputed vile in your sight?
teens.option_b: Speaking blasphemy before the congregation
teens.option_c: Refusing to listen to the wisdom of the aged
teens.option_d: Turning his face away from God in bitterness
teens.explanation: Wherefore are we counted as beasts, and reputed vile in your sight? (Job 18:3).

teens.question: What trap images does Bildad use to describe the fate of the wicked in Job 18:8-10?
teens.option_a: He is cast into a net by his own feet; he walks on a snare; a trap, a robber, and a snare are laid for him
teens.option_b: The wicked man falls into a pit of his own digging
teens.option_c: His enemies set an ambush on his road
teens.option_d: The wicked man is caught by the web of his own lies
teens.explanation: For he is cast into a net by his own feet...The gin shall take him by the heel, the robber shall prevail against him. The snare is laid...and a trap for him in the way (Job 18:8-10).

teens.question: What does Bildad say devours the wicked man's skin in Job 18:13?
teens.option_a: It shall devour the strength of his skin: even the firstborn of death shall devour his strength
teens.option_b: Worms shall eat away his flesh in the grave
teens.option_c: Disease shall spread from his body to all around him
teens.option_d: His own sin consumes him from within
teens.explanation: It shall devour the strength of his skin: even the firstborn of death shall devour his strength (Job 18:13).

teens.question: Where does Bildad say the wicked man is driven in Job 18:14?
teens.option_a: He is rooted out of his tabernacle and brought to the king of terrors
teens.option_b: The wicked man flees from city to city but finds no rest
teens.option_c: He is thrown into the outer darkness forever
teens.option_d: God drives him from the land of the living
teens.explanation: His confidence shall be rooted out of his tabernacle, and it shall bring him to the king of terrors (Job 18:14).

teens.question: What agricultural image does Bildad use for the wickedness of the wicked man's family in Job 18:16?
teens.option_a: His roots shall be dried up beneath, and above shall his branch be cut off
teens.option_b: His vine produces bitter fruit and withers in drought
teens.option_c: His seed falls on rocky ground and bears nothing
teens.option_d: The harvest of his house is taken by strangers
teens.explanation: His roots shall be dried up beneath, and above shall his branch be cut off (Job 18:16).

adults.question: What rhetorical question does Bildad use to rebuke Job in Job 18:4?
adults.option_a: He that teareth himself in his anger: shall the earth be forsaken for thee? and shall the rock be removed out of his place?
adults.option_b: Do you think God changes his laws to suit your complaint?
adults.option_c: Is the Almighty to be moved by the tears of one suffering man?
adults.option_d: Shall wisdom perish because you refuse to hear it?
adults.explanation: He that teareth himself in his anger: shall the earth be forsaken for thee? and shall the rock be removed out of his place? (Job 18:4).

adults.question: How does Bildad describe the dwelling of the wicked in Job 18:6?
adults.option_a: The light shall be dark in his tabernacle, and his candle shall be put out with him
adults.option_b: Shadows fill his house and terror guards his threshold
adults.option_c: The wicked man's home crumbles around him
adults.option_d: Darkness spreads from his door to all his possessions
adults.explanation: The light shall be dark in his tabernacle, and his candle shall be put out with him (Job 18:6).

adults.question: What does Bildad say about the wicked man's own counsel in Job 18:7?
adults.option_a: The steps of his strength shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down
adults.option_b: The wicked man's plans turn against him at every step
adults.option_c: His wisdom fails at the moment he needs it most
adults.option_d: The advice he gives to others destroys him in the end
adults.explanation: The steps of his strength shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down (Job 18:7).

adults.question: What does the phrase "the firstborn of death" in Job 18:13 mean in context?
adults.option_a: The most deadly and dreadful disease, which devours the wicked man's strength
adults.option_b: The firstborn son of the wicked will die first
adults.option_c: Death's firstborn is the angel of destruction
adults.option_d: The first act of God's judgment upon the wicked
adults.explanation: It shall devour the strength of his skin: even the firstborn of death shall devour his strength (Job 18:13) — referring to a most deadly, consuming disease.

adults.question: What does Bildad say dwells in the wicked man's tent in Job 18:15?
adults.option_a: It shall dwell in his tabernacle, because it is none of his: brimstone shall be scattered upon his habitation
adults.option_b: Fear and desolation settle in his home
adults.option_c: His enemies take possession of all he owned
adults.option_d: Darkness and mourning fill every room
adults.explanation: It shall dwell in his tabernacle, because it is none of his: brimstone shall be scattered upon his habitation (Job 18:15).

adults.question: What does Bildad say about the fate of the wicked man's posterity and survivors in Job 18:19?
adults.option_a: He shall neither have son nor nephew among his people, nor any remaining in his dwellings
adults.option_b: His children will bear his shame for three generations
adults.option_c: His descendants will wander homeless among the nations
adults.option_d: No one will build on the ruins of his house
adults.explanation: He shall neither have son nor nephew among his people, nor any remaining in his dwellings (Job 18:19).

adults.question: What reaction do the generations of east and west have to the fate of the wicked in Job 18:20?
adults.option_a: They that come after him shall be astonied at his day, as they that went before were affrighted
adults.option_b: The nations marvel at how swiftly the wicked are destroyed
adults.option_c: All the earth trembles when God punishes the great sinner
adults.option_d: Distant peoples laugh at the fall of the oppressor
adults.explanation: They that come after him shall be astonied at his day, as they that went before were affrighted (Job 18:20).

adults.question: What is the rhetorical effect of Bildad's extended catalogue of disaster in Job 18:5-21, and how does it relate to Job?
adults.option_a: Bildad constructs a detailed picture of the wicked man's fate to imply without naming it that Job's suffering fits this pattern exactly, making his argument a sustained insinuation of guilt
adults.option_b: Bildad is giving a general sermon about divine justice with no specific reference to Job
adults.option_c: Bildad is defending God's goodness against Job's charges of injustice
adults.option_d: Bildad is warning others present not to sin as Job has sinned
adults.explanation: Bildad never names Job directly but builds his catalogue (Job 18:5-20) so that every detail mirrors Job's condition — light put out, driven from his tent, name forgotten — making the chapter a thinly veiled accusation.

kids.question: What great statement of faith did Job make in Job 19:25?
kids.option_a: I know that my redeemer liveth
kids.option_b: God will never leave me alone
kids.option_c: The Lord is my shepherd and helper
kids.option_d: Though I walk through darkness I fear no evil
kids.explanation: For I know that my redeemer liveth, and that he shall stand at the latter day upon the earth (Job 19:25).

kids.question: What does Job ask his friends in Job 19:21?
kids.option_a: Have pity upon me, for the hand of God hath touched me
kids.option_b: Why will you not comfort me in my trouble?
kids.option_c: Do you not see how greatly I suffer?
kids.option_d: Please stop speaking against me
kids.explanation: Have pity upon me, have pity upon me, O ye my friends; for the hand of God hath touched me (Job 19:21).

kids.question: What does Job say he will see after his skin has been destroyed in Job 19:26?
kids.option_a: Yet in my flesh shall I see God
kids.option_b: I will rise and behold my redeemer
kids.option_c: My eyes will see the glory of heaven
kids.option_d: I will look upon the face of the Lord
kids.explanation: And though after my skin worms destroy this body, yet in my flesh shall I see God (Job 19:26).

teens.question: What does Job say God has done to him in Job 19:6?
teens.option_a: God hath overthrown me, and hath compassed me with his net
teens.option_b: God has taken away all my blessings
teens.option_c: God has set my enemies against me like an army
teens.option_d: God has removed his presence far from me
teens.explanation: Know now that God hath overthrown me, and hath compassed me with his net (Job 19:6).

teens.question: How does Job describe his social isolation in Job 19:13-14?
teens.option_a: He hath put my brethren far from me, and mine acquaintance are verily estranged from me; my kinsfolk have failed
teens.option_b: My friends left because of my disease
teens.option_c: The men of my city drove me from the gate
teens.option_d: My enemies spread lies about me until I was alone
teens.explanation: He hath put my brethren far from me, and mine acquaintance are verily estranged from me. My kinsfolk have failed (Job 19:13-14).

teens.question: Who does Job say has become strange to him in Job 19:15-17?
teens.option_a: They that dwell in mine house, my maids, and my own wife
teens.option_b: His former servants and business partners
teens.option_c: The leaders of the city and his allies
teens.option_d: His teachers and the men of his youth
teens.explanation: They that dwell in mine house, and my maids, count me for a stranger...My breath is strange to my wife (Job 19:15-17).

teens.question: What does Job say about children's contempt for him in Job 19:18?
teens.option_a: Yea, young children despised me; I arose and they spake against me
teens.option_b: Even the smallest boys mock me in the street
teens.option_c: Children who knew me before now refuse to greet me
teens.option_d: The sons of the wicked have become my tormentors
teens.explanation: Yea, young children despised me; I arose, and they spake against me (Job 19:18).

teens.question: What does Job say about seeing God on his side in Job 19:27?
teens.option_a: Whom I shall see for myself, and mine eyes shall behold, and not another; though my reins be consumed within me
teens.option_b: God himself will rise and declare my innocence
teens.option_c: I will see my redeemer standing beside me
teens.option_d: My eyes will behold the salvation God has planned
teens.explanation: Whom I shall see for myself, and mine eyes shall behold, and not another; though my reins be consumed within me (Job 19:27).

adults.question: How many times does Job say his friends have wronged and broken him with words in Job 19:2-3?
adults.option_a: Ten times have ye reproached me; ye are not ashamed that ye make yourselves strange to me
adults.option_b: Seven times have they wounded me with their counsel
adults.option_c: More than I can count have I heard your reproaches
adults.option_d: Three times have ye come against me without mercy
adults.explanation: How long will ye vex my soul, and break me in pieces with words? These ten times have ye reproached me (Job 19:2-3).

adults.question: What does Job say about God's seeming indifference to his cry in Job 19:7?
adults.option_a: Behold, I cry out of wrong, but I am not heard: I cry aloud, but there is no judgment
adults.option_b: God has turned his ear away from my prayers
adults.option_c: My supplications rise but find no favour
adults.option_d: The heavens are shut against me
adults.explanation: Behold, I cry out of wrong, but I am not heard: I cry aloud, but there is no judgment (Job 19:7).

adults.question: What does Job say God has done to his path and darkness in Job 19:8?
adults.option_a: He hath fenced up my way that I cannot pass, and he hath set darkness in my paths
adults.option_b: God has removed the light from before me and blocked my way
adults.option_c: The Almighty has shut my mouth and stopped my steps
adults.option_d: God has drawn a line I cannot cross
adults.explanation: He hath fenced up my way that I cannot pass, and he hath set darkness in my paths (Job 19:8).

adults.question: How does Job describe the stripping away of his honour and hope in Job 19:9-10?
adults.option_a: He hath stripped me of my glory and taken the crown from my head. He hath destroyed me on every side, and I am gone; and mine hope hath he removed like a tree
adults.option_b: God has taken away my wealth and left me destitute
adults.option_c: My honour lies in the dust and my enemies tread upon it
adults.option_d: God has silenced my voice and made my name a reproach
adults.explanation: He hath stripped me of my glory, and taken the crown from my head. He hath destroyed me on every side...and mine hope hath he removed like a tree (Job 19:9-10).

adults.question: What is the full three-part confession of faith that Job makes in Job 19:25-27?
adults.option_a: I know my redeemer liveth and shall stand on the earth at the latter day; after worms destroy this body yet in my flesh I shall see God; I shall see him for myself and not another
adults.option_b: God is alive, God will come, and God will vindicate the innocent
adults.option_c: My redeemer is holy, my redeemer is near, and my redeemer will save me
adults.option_d: God knows my name, God hears my cry, and God will answer me in the end
adults.explanation: I know that my redeemer liveth, and...shall stand at the latter day upon the earth: And though after my skin worms destroy this body, yet in my flesh shall I see God: Whom I shall see for myself (Job 19:25-27).

adults.question: What does Job warn his friends about in Job 19:28-29?
adults.option_a: If ye say, How we will persecute him...be ye afraid of the sword: for wrath bringeth the punishments of the sword, that ye may know there is a judgment
adults.option_b: God will turn their own suffering back on them
adults.option_c: Their children will bear the shame of their false counsel
adults.option_d: God will remove their wisdom if they persist in accusation
adults.explanation: But ye should say, Why persecute we him?...Be ye afraid of the sword: for wrath bringeth the punishments of the sword, that ye may know there is a judgment (Job 19:28-29).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the Hebrew word go'el (redeemer/kinsman-redeemer) used in Job 19:25?
adults.option_a: A go'el was a near kinsman who redeemed property or avenged blood; Job uses it of God as one who will personally vindicate his cause and stands as his nearest kin-advocate
adults.option_b: It is simply a poetic name for God without legal meaning
adults.option_c: It refers to an angelic being appointed to defend the righteous
adults.option_d: It is the same word used for a sacrificial priest
adults.explanation: Go'el (redeemer) in Job 19:25 carries the legal force of a kinsman-redeemer who restores, vindicates, and avenges — Job applies this title to God as his personal advocate.

adults.question: How does Job 19:25-27 function as a theological climax within the book and within Old Testament hope?
adults.option_a: It represents the highest point of Job's faith — a resurrection hope that transcends present suffering, and stands as one of the clearest Old Testament affirmations of bodily resurrection and personal vision of God
adults.option_b: It is simply an expression of Job's desire to be vindicated before he dies
adults.option_c: It proves that all Old Testament saints believed in a physical resurrection
adults.option_d: It anticipates only Job's physical restoration in chapter 42
adults.explanation: Job 19:25-27 is widely regarded as a proto-resurrection text — "yet in my flesh shall I see God" — offering bodily, personal, post-mortem hope that resonates with NT resurrection theology (cf. 1 Corinthians 15).

kids.question: Who speaks in Job chapter 20?
kids.option_a: Zophar the Naamathite
kids.option_b: Bildad the Shuhite
kids.option_c: Eliphaz the Temanite
kids.option_d: Job himself
kids.explanation: Then answered Zophar the Naamathite (Job 20:1) — this is Zophar's second speech.

kids.question: What does Zophar say about the triumph of the wicked in Job 20:5?
kids.option_a: The triumphing of the wicked is short, and the joy of the hypocrite but for a moment
kids.option_b: The wicked man prospers all his days but perishes at last
kids.option_c: The glory of the wicked is like the morning cloud
kids.option_d: The wicked man's victory lasts only a season
kids.explanation: That the triumphing of the wicked is short, and the joy of the hypocrite but for a moment (Job 20:5).

kids.question: What does Zophar say God does to the wicked in Job 20:23?
kids.option_a: God shall cast the fury of his wrath upon him
kids.option_b: God will take away the wicked man's children
kids.option_c: God sends a plague to destroy the sinner
kids.option_d: God removes his blessing from the wicked man's house
kids.explanation: When he is about to fill his belly, God shall cast the fury of his wrath upon him, and shall rain it upon him while he is eating (Job 20:23).

teens.question: How does Zophar describe the height to which the wicked man rises before his fall in Job 20:6-7?
teens.option_a: Though his excellency mount up to the heavens and his head reach unto the clouds, yet he shall perish for ever like his own dung
teens.option_b: The wicked man builds himself a great tower and then falls
teens.option_c: His glory is like the eagle's flight before the arrow strikes
teens.option_d: The wicked rise above the mountains only to be cast to the sea
teens.explanation: Though his excellency mount up to the heavens, and his head reach unto the clouds; Yet he shall perish for ever like his own dung (Job 20:6-7).

teens.question: What dream imagery does Zophar use for the wicked in Job 20:8?
teens.option_a: He shall fly away as a dream, and shall not be found: yea, he shall be chased away as a vision of the night
teens.option_b: The wicked man's glory fades like a dream at dawn
teens.option_c: His power passes as quickly as a sleeping man's vision
teens.option_d: The night swallows the wicked as it swallows dreams
teens.explanation: He shall fly away as a dream, and shall not be found: yea, he shall be chased away as a vision of the night (Job 20:8).

teens.question: What does Zophar say about the sweetness of sin in the wicked man's mouth in Job 20:12-13?
teens.option_a: Though wickedness be sweet in his mouth, though he hide it under his tongue and spare it and keep it still within his mouth, yet his meat in his bowels is turned
teens.option_b: The wicked man delights in sin like honey in his mouth
teens.option_c: Sweet sin turns bitter before it is swallowed
teens.option_d: The sinner savours every evil deed like a fine meal
teens.explanation: Though wickedness be sweet in his mouth, though he hide it under his tongue; Though he spare it...yet his meat in his bowels is turned (Job 20:12-14).

teens.question: What does Zophar say the wicked man will vomit up in Job 20:15?
teens.option_a: He hath swallowed down riches, and he shall vomit them up again: God shall cast them out of his belly
teens.option_b: The wicked man spews out all the counsel he has received
teens.option_c: The riches gained by sin become poison in his stomach
teens.option_d: He returns all he has stolen to its rightful owners
teens.explanation: He hath swallowed down riches, and he shall vomit them up again: God shall cast them out of his belly (Job 20:15).

teens.question: What weapon imagery does Zophar use for the fate of the wicked in Job 20:24-25?
teens.option_a: He shall flee from the iron weapon, and the bow of steel shall strike him through; the glittering sword cometh out of his gall
teens.option_b: God's arrows of fire pursue the wicked man to his grave
teens.option_c: The sword of judgment hangs above the sinner's head
teens.option_d: God draws his bow and the wicked man cannot escape
teens.explanation: He shall flee from the iron weapon, and the bow of steel shall strike him through. It is drawn, and cometh out of the body; yea, the glittering sword cometh out of his gall (Job 20:24-25).

adults.question: What motivates Zophar to speak in Job 20:2-3?
adults.option_a: His thoughts cause him to answer because he has heard the check of his reproach and the spirit of his understanding causeth him to answer
adults.option_b: He is angry at Job's latest words
adults.option_c: He has received a new vision from God
adults.option_d: He speaks out of compassion for Job's family
adults.explanation: Therefore do my thoughts cause me to answer, and for this I make haste. I have heard the check of my reproach, and the spirit of my understanding causeth me to answer (Job 20:2-3).

adults.question: What does Zophar say about the eye that saw the wicked man in Job 20:9?
adults.option_a: The eye also which saw him shall see him no more; neither shall his place any more behold him
adults.option_b: Those who loved him will turn their eyes away in shame
adults.option_c: His place in the assembly shall be filled by another
adults.option_d: His family shall forget his face within a generation
adults.explanation: The eye also which saw him shall see him no more; neither shall his place any more behold him (Job 20:9).

adults.question: What does Zophar say about the children of the wicked in Job 20:10?
adults.option_a: His children shall seek to please the poor, and his hands shall restore their goods
adults.option_b: His children inherit his shame and his debt
adults.option_c: The sons of the wicked shall be scattered among the nations
adults.option_d: His children will deny his name to escape punishment
adults.explanation: His children shall seek to please the poor, and his hands shall restore their goods (Job 20:10).

adults.question: What image does Zophar use of the adder's tongue to describe the wicked man's ill-gotten gains in Job 20:16?
adults.option_a: He shall suck the poison of asps: the viper's tongue shall slay him
adults.option_b: The honey of wickedness turns to asp venom in the mouth
adults.option_c: His profits are as deadly as the bite of a serpent
adults.option_d: The wicked man's wine is mixed with the poison of dragons
adults.explanation: He shall suck the poison of asps: the viper's tongue shall slay him (Job 20:16).

adults.question: What does Zophar say about the wicked man's enjoyment of rivers and streams in Job 20:17?
adults.option_a: He shall not see the rivers, the floods, the brooks of honey and butter
adults.option_b: The rivers of blessing flow past his door but he receives nothing
adults.option_c: God turns the streams aside before they reach the sinner
adults.option_d: The wicked man drinks from polluted streams all his days
adults.explanation: He shall not see the rivers, the floods, the brooks of honey and butter (Job 20:17).

adults.question: How does Zophar describe the tent of the wicked in Job 20:26?
adults.option_a: All darkness shall be hid in his secret places: a fire not blown shall consume him; it shall go ill with him that is left in his tabernacle
adults.option_b: His house stands empty in the wilderness
adults.option_c: The wicked man's tent catches fire in the night
adults.option_d: Strangers occupy his dwelling after his death
adults.explanation: All darkness shall be hid in his secret places: a fire not blown shall consume him; it shall go ill with him that is left in his tabernacle (Job 20:26).

adults.question: What cosmic witnesses does Zophar invoke against the wicked in Job 20:27?
adults.option_a: The heaven shall reveal his iniquity; and the earth shall rise up against him
adults.option_b: Sun, moon, and stars testify against the sinner
adults.option_c: God's angels carry the record of every wicked deed
adults.option_d: The mountains and seas will cry out at the last day
adults.explanation: The heaven shall reveal his iniquity; and the earth shall rise up against him (Job 20:27).

adults.question: What is the underlying theological assumption of Zophar's entire speech in chapter 20, and why is it problematic in the context of Job's situation?
adults.option_a: Zophar assumes a strict doctrine of temporal retribution — the wicked always suffer quickly and visibly in this life — which the book of Job is specifically designed to challenge
adults.option_b: Zophar is correct that God always punishes sin in this life
adults.option_c: Zophar's error is only that he applies his doctrine too harshly
adults.option_d: Zophar is presenting Job 20 as a description of the last judgment, not earthly life
adults.explanation: Zophar's speech rests on the assumption that suffering = wickedness and prosperity = righteousness — precisely the oversimplification the book of Job deconstructs, as God himself will rebuke (Job 42:7).

kids.question: What big question does Job ask in Job 21:7?
kids.option_a: Wherefore do the wicked live, become old, yea, are mighty in power?
kids.option_b: Why do the righteous suffer so much?
kids.option_c: When will God answer my prayers?
